Preferred parking /service access is to be designed in consultation with Transport for NSW to ensure the safe and efficient operation of the Parramatta Light Rail.

The former Staff Dining Room and Kitchen (Building C70) and its landscape setting must be conserved and adapted consistent with the Parramatta North Historic Sites Consolidated Conservation Management Plan (PNHS CMP)
New development must be consistent with the requirements of the PNHS CMP for the former Staff Dining Room and Kitchen (Building C70) and its landscape setting and the adjacent Historic Core.
The former Staff Dining Room and Kitchen (Building C70) and its landscape setting must be conserved and adapted as an integral part of Lot F5.
New development must be of an architectural design and character that respects the heritage significance and landscape setting of the heritage building.
New development must provide building setbacks which respond to the context and provide adequate transition to existing built form.
The built form/envelopes of the new development must step down in height adjacent to the heritage building within the Lot (C70) and the adjacent Historic Core.
New development must be aligned to the two streets and reduced in height along the River Road frontage to minimise visual impacts on the heritage buildings and walls of the Historic Core.
The design and treatment of new buildings must have regard to the adjoining planned light rail route to the north.

New development must respond sympathetically to the archaeological remains (if any) of Charles Smith’s Farm and Mrs Bett’s House, including any additional significant archaeological remains in this allotment.
Development must demonstrate compliance with the built form controls indicated on Figure 8.2.2.12.9.1.
A minimum 12 metre wide cross-site pedestrian link is to be provided to conserve the setting of C70 (refer to the PNHS CMP).
A maximum 3 storey street wall is to be provided to River Road with a 4 metre setback for the upper levels to respond to the historic building C106.
The tower setback of new buildings shall respond to the historic building C70.
The building alignment of new buildings is to reflect the C70 building alignment along River Road.
Buildings are to be setback a minimum of 3 metres to the River Road frontage.
Buildings may have a 0 metre setback on Warrinya Avenue. The ground floor is to be recessed by 3 metres to allow courtyard areas. Double storey residential units are preferred on the ground floor.
The adaptative reuse of C70 must facilitate public pedestrian access within its curtilage and remain free of fencing or barriers wherever possible.

Interface between new development and the Parramatta Light Rail Corridor is to be designed in consultation with Council and Transport for NSW to ensure safety for pedestrians, accessibility and visual amenity.

rules.section: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_group: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_text: All significant elements within the Historic Core must be conserved and adapted to sustainable long-term new uses.

rules.section: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_group: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_text: Any new development within the Historic Core is limited to new buildings and structures that are required to support the appropriate and sustainable long-term non-residential use(s) of the significant elements within the Historic Core.

rules.section: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_group: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_text: New development within the Historic Core must be of an architectural design and character that respects the heritage significance and landscape setting of the Historic Core.

rules.section: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_group: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_text: New development must provide building setbacks which respond to the context and provide adequate transition to existing built form.

rules.section: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_group: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_text: New development, including new uses must respond sympathetically to the archaeology of the Historic Core, including any additional significant archaeological remains in this allotment.

rules.section: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_group: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_text: New development must include opportunities for heritage interpretation consistent with the PNUT Heritage Interpretation Strategy.

rules.section: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_group: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_text: New development must include opportunities for through site links and pedestrian access through the Historic Core consistent with the PNHS CMP.

rules.section: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_group: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_text: Any new buildings must demonstrate accordance with the PNHS CMP.

rules.section: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_group: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_text: Development must demonstrate compliance with controls as indicated on Figures 8.2.2.12.10.1 and 8.2.2.12.10.2.

rules.section: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_group: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_text: Any new buildings must demonstrate design excellence by having regard to Clause 6.13 (4) of the Parramatta LEP 2023.

rules.section: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_group: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_text: A minimum 6 metre setback to the south of Bethel House (N06) is to be maintained to any lot boundary.

rules.section: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_group: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_text: The treatment and use of the courtyard areas between historic buildings and structures are to conserve the visual relationship between buildings and facilitate pedestrian activity and activation and reuse of historic buildings.

rules.section: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_group: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_text: Vehicular access and above ground parking should be minimised within the Historic Core.

rules.section: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_group: 8.2.2.12.10 LOTS F6, F7 AND F8 (HISTORIC CORE)
rules.rule_text: Any new development or adaptive reuse of buildings within the Historic Core must demonstrate consideration of its impact on or by the Grey-headed Flying Fox (GHFF) colony location and be consistent with the ecological protection and management requirements of the site.
Any development is to be consistent with the requirements of the Parramatta North Historic Sites Consolidated Conservation Management Plan (PNHS CMP), particularly for the curtilage of Bethel House.
Views from Fennel Street looking west towards Bethel House and the Parramatta River foreshore and beyond must be maintained.
No new development must be located within the curtilage of Bethel House.
Any new development must be located in the south eastern portion of Lot F9 and must step down in scale to the north.

The development potential of the western part of Lot F9 is impacted by the existing endangered Grey-headed Flying Fox (GHFF) colony location. Future development must be consistent with the ecological protection and management requirements of the site.
Views directly down Fennell Street into the Historic Core must be retained at the northern extent of the site. Oblique views into the site from Fleet Street must be retained.
New development must consider views from the World Heritage listed Old Government House and Domain precinct.
A north – south cycling and pedestrian link is to be provided to ensure between Fleet Street, the Parramatta Stadium area and Parramatta Park via the existing pedestrian bridge.
Development must demonstrate compliance with controls as indicated on Figure 8.2.2.12.11.1.
Any boundary to Lot F8 is to be a minimum of 6 metres south of Bethel House.
A minimum building setback of 4 metres to the southern boundary is to be provided.
Significant vegetation south of the Bethel House curtilage shall be retained as shown in Figure 8.2.2.12.11.1 and treated with appropriate supplementary landscaping.
New development shall address the new pedestrian through-site link.
New development must demonstrate that adequate vehicular access is provided through Lot F8 to the north or Eels Place to the south.
The former Nurses Home (Building C83) and its landscape setting must be conserved and adapted consistent with the Parramatta North Historic Sites Consolidated Conservation Management Plan (PNHS CMP).
New development must be consistent with the requirements of the PNHS CMP for the former Nurses Home (Building C83) and its landscape setting.
New development must provide a neighbourhood retail and commercial precinct located on Factory Street.
New development must accommodate the planned light rail route access into the site via Factory Street.
The former Nurses Home (Building C83) and its landscape setting must be conserved and adapted as an integral part of Lot G1.

New development within G1 must be of an architectural design and character that respects the heritage significance and landscape setting of the heritage building (C83) and the adjacent Parramatta Gaol.
New development must create a new neighbourhood retail precinct for the PNUT area and have an urban character.
The new neighbourhood retail precinct must allow for and integrate with the planned light rail route along Factory Street.
New development must provide building and tower setbacks which provide adequate transition to existing built form.
The surrounding public domain shall be of high-quality and allow for wide footpaths and the establishment of street trees.
The built form/envelopes of the new development must step down in height adjacent to the Nurses Home (Building C83) and the pedestrian through-site link.
New development must provide a publicly accessible east-west through site pedestrian link.
New development must locate retail and/or active uses along the Factory Street frontages and the through-site link.
Any taller built form must be located to reinforce the New Street alignment and the built form/envelope must step down in height towards the historic Parramatta Gaol and Factory Street.
No vehicular access must be provided from Dunlop Street or Eastern Circuit East. Vehicular access is preferred from New Street.
Active retail frontages along Factory Street must include weather protection in the form of awnings and/or colonnade treatments.

Basement parking may extend beyond the building footprint subject to an assessment of the landscape impacts and demonstration that areas available for deep soil planting are maximised wherever possible.
Development must demonstrate compliance with controls as indicated on Figure 8.2.2.12.12.1.
A 0 metre building setback is required where active commercial/retail uses are proposed along Factory Street, as indicated on Figure 8.2.2.6.1.
Active commercial/retail uses must provide awnings and designed to make allowance for the full extent of mature street tree canopies.
Development along Dunlop Street must:
Development along Eastern Circuit East must:

Where the minimum footpath standards cannot be meet along Dunlop Street and Eastern Circuit East, the building setback control shall be consistent with the 3 metre building setback control in Section 8.2.2.10 of this DCP.
Development along New Street must provide a 3 metre building setback.
A link from the East-West Link is to be provided to connect to Factory Street within the curtilage of the Nurses Home.
A 16 metre wide east-west cross-site pedestrian link from New Street to Eastern Circuit East is to be provided. This must be open to the sky.
A minimum 16 metre setback is to be provided between the Nurses Home (Building C83) and any new building.

The adaptive reuse of the Nurses Home (Building C83) must facilitate pedestrian public access within its curtilage (to the North, West and South) and remain free of fencing or barriers wherever possible. No above ground parking is allowed.
New development should facilitate a neighbourhood retail and commercial precinct on Factory Street.
New development should improve the quality of the interface with the adjoining open space.
New development must define street edges and the Factory Street entry point to the PNUT.
Any taller built form must be located to reinforce the north eastern corner and the built form must step down to reduce overshadowing on the Public Open Space (OS4).
New development must create a new neighbourhood retail precinct for the PNUT area and have an urban character.
The new neighbourhood retail precinct must allow for and integrate with the planned light rail route along Factory Street.
Setbacks to the open space are to include landscaped courtyards and/or active uses.

Basement parking may extend beyond the building footprint subject to an assessment of the landscape impacts and demonstration that areas available for deep soil planting are maximised wherever possible.
Development must demonstrate compliance with controls as indicated on Figures 8.2.2.12.13.1 and 8.2.2.12.13.2.
New development must provide active retail frontages to Factory Street and Fleet Street.
CA 0 metre building setback is allowed where active commercial/retail uses are proposed along Factory Street, as indicated on Figure 8.2.2.6.1.
Vehicular access is to be from Fleet Street to avoid disrupting the Factory Street retail frontage.
New development to provide a minimum 3 metre upper level building setback to street frontages and rear and side boundaries.
The western edge of the new built form must align with the western façade of the Nurses Home across Factory Street.

New development must maximise the interface with the Public Open Space (OS4) provide active ground level frontages and opportunities for casual surveillance from apartment building facades (refer to Figure 8.2.2.12.13.1).
New development must demonstrate, through design testing, that overshadowing impacts to open space are minimised.
Development is to retain and respond to the historic Quarry Face and retain the early sandstone walling along Fleet and Albert Streets.
Development is to integrate and transition to surrounding urban development.
New development within H1, H2, H3, H4 and H5 must retain the sandstone quarry face and early sandstone walls to Fleet and Albert Streets.
New buildings must be designed to interpret the change in level and allow some views of the quarry face from within the lot and from the Albert Street steps.

Any taller built form must be located in the southern portion of the lot to reinforce the Fleet Street/Albert Street corner with heights transitioning down to adjacent existing development to the north.
Development must demonstrate compliance with controls as indicated on Figure 8.2.2.12.14.1. Setbacks
New development must provide building setbacks which respond to the context and provide adequate transition to existing built form.
Buildings are to be setback a minimum of 4 metres from Fleet Street.
Buildings are to be setback a minimum of 6 metres from Albert Street.

The 4 metre building setback from Fleet Street is required to provide landscaped courtyards to ground floor units and the 6 metre building setback to Albert Street is to respond to the landscape quality of that street.
New development within H1, H2, H3, H4 and H5 must retain the sandstone quarry face and early sandstone walls to Fleet and Albert Streets.
New development must be designed to interpret the change in level and allow some views of the quarry face from within the lots and from the Albert Street steps.
New development must provide building setbacks which respond to the context and provide adequate transition to existing built form.
New development may include a pedestrian through-site link connecting O’Connell Street with Fleet Street and transitioning across the quarry face.
New development within the lots must comprise a series of high-quality apartment buildings with a diversity of scale and architectural character.
Low rise buildings (6 storeys maximum) must define the external street edges with the exception of Albert Street where 8 storey buildings may be developed.
Tall buildings must be located internally and minimise overshadowing to existing and new development. Towers must be expressed above 6 storey podiums, which must define public and communal spaces.
All new buildings to have addresses and lobbies with access to a public street or through site link.
Development fronting Fleet Street must respect the visual relationship with the Historic Core to the west.
Development must demonstrate compliance with controls as indicated on Figure 8.2.2.12.15.1.
A cross-site pedestrian link is to be provided centrally between O’Connell Street and Fleet Street to align with Harold Street.
A minimum 4 metre building setback to Fleet Street is to be provided.
A minimum 6 metre building setback to Albert, O’Connell and Fennel Streets is to be provided.
All new buildings are to address public streets.

Develop a mixed use core of retail, residential and business at the transport node serving the precinct, centred around Good Street, Cowper Street and Rowell Street, and extends to the north side of Parramatta Road.
Ensure new development within the mixed use core provides active ground floor uses to increase vibrancy, safety, use and interest of the area,
Preserve and improve significant open space areas within the precinct.
Maximise pedestrian links and connectivity through new laneways and through site links.
Ensure that new development provides a strong interface to Granville Railway Station, Parramatta Road and Good Street.
Ensure that new development responds well to existing heritage items.
Ensure new development within the mixed use area provides active ground floor uses to increase the safety, use and interest of the area.
Ensure new buildings within the mixed use area provide articulation and an attractive composition of building elements.
Enhance residential amenity through provision of landscaping and communal open space at ground level.
Ensure an appropriate height transition of building heights to maintain amenity of adjacent development.

New pedestrian connections, roads and laneways should be provided in accordance with Figure 8.2.3.1.1.2. Where a development provides for public access connections, a variation to Council’s floor space ratio control can be sought in accordance with C.02 below.

Where a development provides for dedication of land to Council for the purposes of providing public access and the construction of the access way, Council may consider increasing the maximum floor space ratio. As a guide, the maximum floor space ratio may be increased by the equivalent area represented by 50% of the land area to be dedicated to Council for the public access. The site area may include the area of land to be dedicated to Council for the purpose of the floor space ratio calculation. The proposed variation to floor space is to be addressed under Clause 4.6 ‘Exceptions to development standards’ in the Parramatta LEP 2023.

New road connections and laneways should be provided to improve through block connections, remove dead end streets, extend existing connections, improve serviceability of retail development and improve the interface to the railway line.
New street links are to match the width of the existing public road that it forms and extension of. New laneways are to have a minimum width of 6 metres.
New pedestrian links are to improve through block connections and provide better links to and from Granville Railway Station.
New pedestrian connections are to have a minimum width of 3 metres and are to be consistent in width for their full length.
Front building setbacks are to be in accordance with Figure 8.2.3.1.1.2 and any additional controls set out below:
Side and rear building setbacks are to be in accordance with Figure 8.2.3.1.1.2 and the below controls:
To achieve a continuous street edge development in the E1 Local Centre zone should have a nil side setback where it will not have a detrimental impact upon adjoining development.
Building setbacks to existing and desired laneways should be designed to activate the laneway while still allowing for the servicing needs of development.

Where development proposes of adjoins residential development greater than 2 storeys in height, building separation requirements prescribed by the Apartment Design Guide published by the NSW Department of Planning and Environment should be achieved.
The building separation distances between buildings on the same site are not to be less than those required between buildings on adjoining sites.

The minimum site frontage for development in MU1 Mixed Use zone or E3 Productivity Support zone on land between Parramatta Road and the railway line is to be in accordance with the following table: Table 8.2.3.1.1 – Minimum site frontage
The preferred pattern of land amalgamation is to be side by side to maximise lineal street frontage and to encourage east west built form for good solar access, as shown in Figure 8.2.3.1.1.4.
In the E3 Productivity Support zone along Parramatta Road, a minimum of 20% of the site is to be a deep soil zone.
In the MU1 Mixed Use zone between Parramatta Road and railway line, a minimum of 30% of the site is to be a deep soil zone, and not less than 40% of the site is to be landscaped.
The required deep soil areas are to be predominantly located at the rear of the site to provide a landscape corridor and visual screening between buildings.

Where a front building setback is required as shown in Figure 8.2.3.1.1.2 (with the exception of Parramatta Road), the front setback area is to be landscaped. Provision of street trees is required in this area.
For development fronting Parramatta Road, the setback area is to form an extension of the footway. Landscape planting including street trees is encouraged.

rules.rule_title: Development between Parramatta Road and Railway Line
rules.rule_text: Residential and commercial apartments are to be designed to enable casual surveillance of public spaces.

rules.rule_title: Development between Parramatta Road and Railway Line
rules.rule_text: For development greater than 15 metres in height, buildings with large floor plates, must be expressed as separate building elements.

rules.rule_title: Development between Parramatta Road and Railway Line
rules.rule_text: For development greater than 15 metres in height the horizontal dimension of any building façade must not exceed 35 metres.

rules.rule_title: Development between Parramatta Road and Railway Line
rules.rule_text: For development greater than 15 metres in height the maximum floor plate area of a nonresidential buildings is 480m², with a maximum depth of 25 metres.

rules.rule_title: Development between Parramatta Road and Railway Line
rules.rule_text: For commercial buildings, the maximum building depth is 25 metres.

rules.rule_title: Development between Parramatta Road and Railway Line
rules.rule_text: Use light wells and courtyards to improve internal building amenity and cross ventilation.

rules.rule_title: Development between Parramatta Road and Railway Line
rules.rule_text: The roof forms of all buildings are to add interest to the skyline.

rules.rule_title: Development between Parramatta Road and Railway Line
rules.rule_text: Storeys above the first four storeys of the proposed development shall have an additional 3.1 metres upper level rear setback and the proposed development’s rear building setback (facing the low density residential area) is to be a minimum of 9 metres (without the rear existing laneway).

rules.rule_title: Development between Parramatta Road and Railway Line
rules.rule_text: The proposed development at 2-22 William Street, is to be not more than 5 storeys.
That buildings and structures adjoining the Parramatta River contribute to the attractive appearance of the foreshore and do not dominate the skyline in views along the Parramatta River.
Buildings should make a positive contribution to the streetscape and the skyline.
Create a strong and unified character along the major gateways into Parramatta.
That buildings that not significantly overshadow the public domain, vegetated riparian areas, environmental protection areas or adjoining properties.
Conserve heritage sites, their settings, identified views and their visual interconnections.
Development must not have an adverse impact on significant or historic views from or of heritage sites along the Parramatta River when seen from river and nearby historic sites.

Any development within the Rydalmere Precinct and on land shown on the Camellia Design Control Map as “Area of Height Sensitivity” must demonstrate through survey and photo montages, that the height of the proposed development does not have a significant adverse impact on identified views to the Female Orphan School (University of Western Sydney Rydalmere Campus) and its emergent trees, the Parramatta River Corridor and Pennant Hills open space ridge line. The relevant identified views for the Camellia and Rydalmere precincts are provided in Appendix 1.
Enhance the appearance of these precincts and the setting of heritage items or areas, particularly from the waterway, major thoroughfares, and any other public places.
Protect and enhance the riparian ecosystem along the Parramatta River and its tributaries.
Improve environmental performance, particularly in terms of water management, pollution control, the natural environment, biodiversity, energy efficiency and transport management.
provide for recreational use of the foreshore and establishment of paths for walking and cycling where these will not diminish natural values.

Development must improve the foreshore landscape so that locally native vegetation and natural geomorphology are preserved, restored and extended and in accordance with any Government-adopted catchment strategies.
Any fencing is to be set back from the property boundary and screened in front by locally native and local provenance trees and shrubs.

Except where identified as culturally significant heritage landscape, the proposed landscaping is to be consist of plants that are local to the area, especially for the foreshore of the Parramatta River and tributaries, and of local provenance, and are to be planted in an appropriate vegetation sequence.

Open storage areas, material handling areas and car parking are to be located away from any boundaries that border on public areas, particularly the foreshore of the Parramatta River and its tributaries, and major transport routes.
Vegetated buffers are to be provided around areas of open storage or material handling, to soften the visual impacts and reduce dust and stormwater runoff.
Redevelopment of land adjacent to waterways must make provision for landscaped corridors that enhance the natural values of the foreshore ecosystem.

The landscape setbacks along major streets and riparian vegetation along the rivers and creeks are to be in accordance with the Camellia and Rydalmere Precinct Design Control Map, with the exception of any riparian vegetation area along the Parramatta River for the University of Western Sydney site, which may be varied provided there is a Conservation Management Plan for the site and the redevelopment achieves all the outcomes specified for the University Special Area.
A landscape management plan and strategy is to be developed to ensure continuity and attractiveness of landscaping.

rules.section: 8.2.4.4 TRAVEL PLANS AND TRAVEL INFORMATION GUIDES
rules.rule_group: 8.2.4.4 TRAVEL PLANS AND TRAVEL INFORMATION GUIDES
rules.rule_text: Development that contains 5,000m² of gross floor space or 50 or more employees must prepare a Travel Plan.

rules.section: 8.2.4.4 TRAVEL PLANS AND TRAVEL INFORMATION GUIDES
rules.rule_group: 8.2.4.4 TRAVEL PLANS AND TRAVEL INFORMATION GUIDES
rules.rule_text: A Travel Plan must include:

rules.section: 8.2.4.4 TRAVEL PLANS AND TRAVEL INFORMATION GUIDES
rules.rule_group: 8.2.4.4 TRAVEL PLANS AND TRAVEL INFORMATION GUIDES
rules.rule_text: All other developments may be required to prepare a traveller information guide that provides detailed information about all public transport services, pedestrian paths, cycle ways and ferry timetables in the area that would be used to actively encourage employees to use public transport to and from the Camellia and Rydalmere Precincts.
Provide opportunities for casual surveillance of the streetscape and public domain.
Improve architectural interest by minimising the bulk of buildings and to encourage articulation and modulation of development.
Development that respects, conserves and responds to identified views and the existing heritage character of the precinct.

Development is to contribute to improved amenity, safety and appearance of the public domain through landscaping, building setbacks, attractive and clearly defined entrances to sites and buildings, and clear and attractive signage.
Major facade and entries of buildings are to address major public places, including roads, parks and waterways.
Development is to have regard to adjoining building works and transition of height, massing and scale.
Building setbacks, design, materials, glazing and colours are to minimise the visual impact of the development, particularly if the development is visible from roads and the Parramatta River.
Buildings on sites adjacent to the Parramatta River and its tributaries are to be set back in accordance with any foreshore building line.
Building bulk created by large unbroken expanses of wall is to be reduced by articulation and modulation, particularly where facing a public place such as a street, a park or the Parramatta River.
Buildings are not to overshadow environmental protection areas or riparian vegetation areas.
Lighting is not to have adverse impact on the natural habitats.

Open storage areas, material handling areas and car parking are to be located away from any boundaries that border on public areas, particularly the foreshore of the Parramatta River and its tributaries, and major transport routes.
Building roofs and lift overrun structures are to be dark and have matt colours so as to be recessive.

rules.section: 8.2.4.7.1 THE JAMES RUSE DRIVE CORRIDOR SPECIAL AREA
rules.rule_group: 8.2.4.7.1 THE JAMES RUSE DRIVE CORRIDOR SPECIAL AREA
rules.rule_text: Development must contribute to a strong, unified and visually attractive character for James Ruse Drive, enhancing its role as an important gateway to Parramatta.

rules.section: 8.2.4.7.1 THE JAMES RUSE DRIVE CORRIDOR SPECIAL AREA
rules.rule_group: 8.2.4.7.1 THE JAMES RUSE DRIVE CORRIDOR SPECIAL AREA
rules.rule_text: Best available construction materials, design techniques, finishes and interior layouts should be used to minimise the potential environmental impacts arising from James Ruse Drive and the rail line.

rules.section: 8.2.4.7.1 THE JAMES RUSE DRIVE CORRIDOR SPECIAL AREA
rules.rule_group: 8.2.4.7.1 THE JAMES RUSE DRIVE CORRIDOR SPECIAL AREA
rules.rule_text: Development has vehicular access via local roads and not directly off James Ruse Drive.

rules.section: 8.2.4.7.1 THE JAMES RUSE DRIVE CORRIDOR SPECIAL AREA
rules.rule_group: 8.2.4.7.1 THE JAMES RUSE DRIVE CORRIDOR SPECIAL AREA
rules.rule_text: Management of the traffic impacts of development on James Ruse Drive.

rules.section: 8.2.4.7.1 THE JAMES RUSE DRIVE CORRIDOR SPECIAL AREA
rules.rule_group: 8.2.4.7.1 THE JAMES RUSE DRIVE CORRIDOR SPECIAL AREA
rules.rule_text: Integration of development with public transport.

rules.section: 8.2.4.7.1 THE JAMES RUSE DRIVE CORRIDOR SPECIAL AREA
rules.rule_group: 8.2.4.7.1 THE JAMES RUSE DRIVE CORRIDOR SPECIAL AREA
rules.rule_text: Land within proximity of the proposed Sydney West metro station is to be developed with consideration of the following:

rules.section: 8.2.4.7.1 THE JAMES RUSE DRIVE CORRIDOR SPECIAL AREA
rules.rule_group: 8.2.4.7.1 THE JAMES RUSE DRIVE CORRIDOR SPECIAL AREA
rules.rule_text: New development along this corridor needs to be carefully planned and based on the following design principles:

rules.section: 8.2.4.7.1 THE JAMES RUSE DRIVE CORRIDOR SPECIAL AREA
rules.rule_group: 8.2.4.7.1 THE JAMES RUSE DRIVE CORRIDOR SPECIAL AREA
rules.rule_text: Any development undertaken for Rosehill Racecourse, will require the preparation of a detailed structure plan prepared by the proponent. Emphasis of the Structure Plan should be on meeting key tourism objectives, improving the physical appearance of development along James Ruse Drive, and to demonstrate positive measures to manage traffic issues and encourage public transport use.
Buildings must have high-quality finishes where visible from the street and a high-quality frontage with landscaping.

Signage has a high standard and provides clear information as to the use of the land, the street address and clearly marked entrance and exit ways and is of a scale and nature sympathetic to the building form.

Where a property adjoins a natural waterway, the land is revegetated with locally native flora where possible and any area adjacent to the foreshore is maintained so as to limit run-off and such areas are considered for outdoor recreation or lunch areas.
The landscape setbacks shown on the Design Control Map in this Section are to be met.
Development must conserve and enhance items of heritage significance consistent with a Conservation Management Plan for the area.
Development must respect, conserve and respond to key views identified in that Plan.
Development must protect and enhance cultural plantings and native bushland and other natural features along the foreshore.
Development must provide for public access along the foreshore.
The scale and character of the development must recognise and complement the unique visual qualities of the site.
Development should integrate with the public transport network and facilitate access for pedestrians and cyclists to the site and, where appropriate, through the site.
The siting and design of the development must minimise adverse effects from adjoining land uses, including noise from James Ruse Drive.
Development must enhance the key approach routes to Parramatta, being James Ruse Drive, Victoria Road, the rail line and Parramatta River.
A maximum site coverage of 80% shall be permitted on site.
The following setbacks shall apply:
Loading areas shall be screened from public roads and public access areas.
Active and defined frontages
Entrances and windows shall be located on the ground floor of the building to face the public domain and created visual surveillance.
Buildings on street corners or the interface with public space shall emphasise the corner by appropriate architectural treatment.
All building materials used shall be durable, low maintenance and of high-quality. Pedestrian amenity
Public pedestrian networks within sites shall provide solar, wind and rain protection using a colonnade, an awning or other appropriate shading devices.
Awnings/colonnades in buildings shall be designed to a height of 3.5 metres.
Signage shall be located below the awning height of a building. Parking rates
Bicycle parking requirement is 1 bicycle space per 300m² of retail space. Car Parking
Minimum of 1 car space per 38m² GFA
Stairwells shall be positioned to create a stack effect to enhance natural ventilation and remove warm summer air from upper floors.
Plant types shall be selected so as not to overshadow potential location of rooftop solar collectors.
Refer to Part 5 – Environmental Management of this DCP for other development controls for energy efficiency and water conservation.
Plant species that are drought tolerant or will require minimal watering once established shall be used.

Water-conserving landscape practices shall be applied where possible, including soil amendment, mulch, irrigation zoning, limited turf areas, planting in relation to micro-climate, water scheduling and selection of plants with water needs that match site rainfall and drainage conditions.
No imported topsoil shall be used. Stockpile and rehabilitate existing topsoil on site.
Landscape plant species used in the public domain shall be predominantly native, including local indigenous species.
Native ground covers and grasses shall be used in lieu of turf where practicable.
Where appropriate, street corners and main entry points shall be emphasised by appropriate architectural treatment.
Setbacks shall be designed to provide for a street edge defined by built form and landscape treatment, with minimum setbacks to active façade zones, and increased setbacks to solid walls.

Streetscapes shall be treated as active zones. Where possible, entry and office facades shall be orientated to the street. Other façade zones, such as setback solid walls, shall be treated with landscape areas to provide shade and amenity as well as visual interest to the streetscape.

Where buildings are setback to allow for car parking at entry zones, street edges shall be designed with permeable landscape buffers to permit street address/exposure, whilst maintaining defined edges.
The total site coverage shall not exceed 60% of the area of the allotment.
Building setbacks: Holker Street: 4 metres minimum
Service areas generally shall not detract from the character of the public street.
10% of the site area shall be soft landscaping. Architectural Elements
Building facades and/or landscape treatment shall create a defined edge to the Newington Small Village boulevard and primary public streets.

Identification signs shall be integrated with the building design or within the landscape zone not higher than 2.4 metres above ground. All signage shall be permitted to be illuminated as per the relevant Australian Standards.
Ensure that development provides a degree of consistency to establish the neighbourhood character of the precinct.
Ensure that the appearance of the development is of high visual quality and enhances and addresses the street.
Ensure that the form, scale and height of proposed development protects the amenity of adjoining properties and the locality.
Ensure that the form, scale and height of the proposed development responds appropriately to site characteristics.
Ensure that the development is designed, detailed and finished to provide an appropriate scale to the street.
Ensure that new development relates well to surrounding development.
Ensure that when ‘built out’ the precinct provides a high-quality sustainable environment.
All buildings shall address the street.
Where a building has two street frontages, it shall address the primary (major) street.
Stepped building arrangements may be encouraged where narrow lot types reinforce the street.
Entrances to residential flat buildings shall be clear and legible from the street.
The storey height shall be controlled in residential areas to avoid overshadowing of neighbouring private open spaces.
Car court arrangements shall ensure that a minimum of 60% of dwellings have garages at rear.
Where private open space is located on the street frontage, 2 metre walls shall be permitted for a maximum of 60% of frontage.
Driveways and high fences shall be paired where possible. Stormwater drainage
Applicants shall consult the Part 5 – Environmental Management of this DCP for Stormwater Drainage.
Applicants shall consult the Section 5.4.9 of this DCP for waste requirements. Landscaping
Applicants shall refer to the relevant provision within the Section 6.4 – Loading and Servicing of this DCP.
Applicants shall consult the Parramatta Public Domain Guideline for all public domain requirements.
Applicants shall consult the relevant provisions within Section 2.11 – Access for People with a Disability of this DCP.
Applicants shall consult the relevant provisions of the Multi Dwelling or the Residential Flat Building sections within Part 3 – Residential Development of this DCP.
A minimum area of 25m² of continuous open space relating to living areas shall be provided on each site.
Major open space shall be screened if located at the front of the building. Privacy
Visual privacy shall be required and may be achieved by:
For acoustic privacy, buildings shall:
Single lot dwelling living spaces shall be orientated within 20 degrees west of North and 30 degrees east of North and open directly onto north facing private open space where possible.
North facing external walls to living areas of dwellings shall achieve 2 hours of solar access in mid-winter.

Single lot dwellings shall be designed to ensure minimum of 2 hours of solar access to a minimum of 50% of the required provision of adjacent private outdoor living space between 9:00am and 3:00pm midwinter.

North facing windows shall be maximised and have horizontal protection to ensure shading of glazing occurs when the midday sun angle is 65 degrees or more. Shading devices to north facing windows shall provide sun penetration when the midday sun angle is 34 degrees or less.
Window sizes shall be minimized when facing south, west and east or shall be installed with vertical screens or solar film to west and east facing windows.
High mass materials shall be used where possible.

Building heights shall reinforce the scale and quality of the streetscape within Newington’s residential precinct and surroundings. To allow flexibility, sloping sites shall have a maximum building height of two storeys plus attic.
Front fencing shall be 50% transparent. The minimum requirement for front fencing shall be 900mm. The maximum front fencing height requirement shall be 1.2mm.
Dividing fences shall be a maximum of 1.8 metres in height and, where required, shall allow for surveillance of street.
Privacy shall be provided to private open space where it abuts the street. Surveillance via the entry and living/kitchen room windows shall be maintained.
Design of wall/fence shall be integrated with the design of the building and shall allow for penetration of breezes.
Where surveillance of the street or open space is required, fences shall be permeable. Refer to Table 8.5.3.2.1 for building materials, colours and finishes.
Garden walls (semi-transparent) shall be a minimum height of 1.5 metres and a maximum height of 2 metres.

Garden walls shall be permitted to the street only when enclosing north facing private residential open space with a maximum width of 60% of street frontage and a maximum unarticulated length of 12 metres wide residential areas.
Garden walls shall not obscure the view of the front door from the street. Refer to Table 8.5.3.2.1 for garden wall materials, colours and finishes of this Part.
Garages and carports shall read as secondary to building forms and compatible with the architectural character. Refer to Table 8.5.3.2.1 for materials, colours and finishes for carports and garages.

Dimensions of pergolas as shown below shall be appropriate to the function and design of pergolas shall be integral with architectural design of the building. Refer to Table 8.5.3.2.1 for building materials, colours and finishes. Height Maximum 3 metres Depth Minimum 1 metre and 3 metres maximum Width 2 metres over outdoor living areas
Pergolas shall provide shade when sun is above 65 degrees or more (summer) and when the sun angle is 34 degrees or less (winter).
Refer Table 8.5.3.2.1 for development controls and finishes relating to decks, porches, terraces and verandahs.
Refer to Table 8.5.3.2.1 for materials, sizes and finishes for various building elements associated with the development.

rules.rule_text: Maximum dwelling height shall be 2 storeys plus attic. Dwelling setback at upper levels shall be determined by overshadowing of the adjoining block. Single storey shall be in rear 50% of site. Note: Figure 8.2.5.3.4.7 illustrates the site layout for a two storey terrace house.

rules.section: 8.2.5.3.4 SINGLE LOT HOUSING TYPESDETACHED FAMILY DWELLING – STANDARD LOT
rules.rule_group: 8.2.5.3.4 SINGLE LOT HOUSING TYPESDETACHED FAMILY DWELLING – STANDARD LOT
rules.rule_text: Minimum lot width and depth shall be 6 metres and 20 metres. Siting

rules.section: 8.2.5.3.4 SINGLE LOT HOUSING TYPESDETACHED FAMILY DWELLING – STANDARD LOT
rules.rule_group: 8.2.5.3.4 SINGLE LOT HOUSING TYPESDETACHED FAMILY DWELLING – STANDARD LOT
rules.rule_text: Living areas shall face north. Minimum 25m² shall be required as private open space with direct access to living area. Dwelling entry shall be clearly visible from street.

rules.section: 8.2.5.3.4 SINGLE LOT HOUSING TYPESDETACHED FAMILY DWELLING – STANDARD LOT
rules.rule_group: 8.2.5.3.4 SINGLE LOT HOUSING TYPESDETACHED FAMILY DWELLING – STANDARD LOT
rules.rule_title: Solar amenity and private open space
rules.rule_text: Minimum 50% of private open space area shall be required and north facing living area wall shall have a minimum of 2 hours solar access during mid-winter.

rules.section: 8.2.5.3.4 SINGLE LOT HOUSING TYPESDETACHED FAMILY DWELLING – STANDARD LOT
rules.rule_group: 8.2.5.3.4 SINGLE LOT HOUSING TYPESDETACHED FAMILY DWELLING – STANDARD LOT
rules.rule_text: Front setback shall be a minimum of 1.5 metres and 3 metres at a collector street. 50% of frontage shall be within a 5 metres building alignment zone.

rules.section: 8.2.5.3.4 SINGLE LOT HOUSING TYPESDETACHED FAMILY DWELLING – STANDARD LOT
rules.rule_group: 8.2.5.3.4 SINGLE LOT HOUSING TYPESDETACHED FAMILY DWELLING – STANDARD LOT
rules.rule_text: Rear setback shall be 3 metres minimum or 12 metres where site is adjacent to western industrial development area.

rules.section: 8.2.5.3.4 SINGLE LOT HOUSING TYPESDETACHED FAMILY DWELLING – STANDARD LOT
rules.rule_group: 8.2.5.3.4 SINGLE LOT HOUSING TYPESDETACHED FAMILY DWELLING – STANDARD LOT
rules.rule_text: Side setback shall be zero lot lined. Where it is not zero lot lined, side setback shall be a minimum of 1 metre.

rules.section: 8.2.5.3.4 SINGLE LOT HOUSING TYPESDETACHED FAMILY DWELLING – STANDARD LOT
rules.rule_group: 8.2.5.3.4 SINGLE LOT HOUSING TYPESDETACHED FAMILY DWELLING – STANDARD LOT
rules.rule_text: Maximum dwelling height shall be 2 storeys plus attic. Dwelling setback at upper levels shall be determined by overshadowing of adjoining block. Single storey shall be in rear 50% of site.
Site planning shall maximise the northern aspect for a majority of units of the development. Site coverage
The maximum site coverage shall be 60%. Private open space
The above ground floor level shall have a 7m² minimum area of private open space for a balcony and a minimum width of 1.8 metres.
The ground floor level shall have 10m² minimum area of private open space per dwelling and a minimum dimension of 3 metres.
Visual privacy shall be required and may be achieved by:
For acoustic privacy, buildings shall:
Building heights shall be set to reinforce the scale and quality of the streetscape. A maximum building height of 4 storeys (residential) for a residential flat building shall be permitted.
Fencing or screens shall be used to maintain privacy between balconies/terraces.
Fencing or screens shall not be permitted to lot boundaries unless for security or privacy or to screen service areas or equipment.
Surveillance of the street shall be allowed where required.
Privacy shall be provided to ground floor private open space where it abuts the street. Surveillance via the entry and living/kitchen room windows to be maintained.
Design of the wall/fence shall be integrated with the design of the building.
Front walls/fences unless enclosing private open space shall be a maximum of 1.2 metres in height.

The front and side dividing fences, where located within the front yard area, shall not exceed a height of 1.2 metres as measured above existing ground level and shall be a minimum of 50% transparent. Front and side dividing fences where located within the front yard area shall not be constructed of solid pre-coated metal type materials such as Colorbond or similar.

Front walls/fences shall be a maximum of 1.8 metres high if enclosing private residential open space with a maximum width of 60% of street frontage and a maximum unarticulated length of 12 metres in residential development.
Refer to Table 8.5.3.2.1 for materials, finishes and colours. Lot size
Minimum lot width shall be 40 metres and minimum lot depth shall be 25 metres. Siting
Minimum 10m² private open space with direct access to living areas shall be required at ground floor levels.
Minimum 7m² private open space with direct access to living areas shall be required at upper floor levels. Building entry shall be clearly visible from the street.
The following setbacks shall apply:
Residential flat building shall be a maximum of 4 storeys above ground level (existing), as shown in Figure 8.2.5.3.5.2. Setback at upper levels is determined by overshadowing of adjoining block.
Create a legible, coherent, and attractive suburb characterised by generous, diverse streets and public spaces reinforced by the built form and vegetation.
Organise the buildings so that they form a coherent outcome; address and define the streets, pedestrian connections, courtyards, and special places.
Ensure that the spaces of the public domain - streets, squares, and parks are of high quality and amenity.
Facilitate sustainable, resilient buildings that address climate, topography, energy consumption, urban heat, pedestrian scale, and internal amenity.
Protect and improve the natural environment and biodiversity.
Provide sufficient detail of Council requirements and expectations to enable Development Applications to be easily assessed.
Safely manage overland flow and stormwater through the site and broader precinct, and design buildings and landscape accordingly.
Ensure that infrastructure is delivered in accordance with the staging plan and TMAP Implementation Plan.

An infrastructure Development Application (Development Application) is required to be lodged for the entire precinct upfront prior to individual Development Applications being lodged on a site-by-site basis, detailing the following:
Ensure that development individually and collectively contributes to the architectural and overall urban design quality of Melrose Park.
Incorporate design quality in public and private development as a central consideration through all stages of the process from design to completion.
Ensure that the integrity of design quality is carried through to the construction and completion of developments.
Incorporate overall coherence of the architecture within the whole precinct with variety in the detailed architectural resolution.
All Design Competition briefs must contain a reference to the objectives and controls in this DCP and be prepared in accordance with Council's Design Excellence Competition Manual.
Architectural Reference Designs developed as part of a Design Competition brief should use this DCP as the basis for building envelopes.
This DCP should form the primary basis of assessment of all Design Excellence Competition winning schemes.

For all Development Applications in Melrose Park that are not subject to a Design Competition, the Architect should provide sufficient detailed documentation for the building façades and external areas to form part of the consent documents. These should include fully annotated 1:20 scale cross-sections and partial plans of façades, details of typical and important junctions, and details and materials specification of all external works.
The Landscape Architect and Civil Engineer for all Development Applications must provide fully coordinated Public Domain Alignment Drawings (refer to Section 8.2.6.3 – Public Domain).
A variety in the detailed design of individual buildings shall be provided.
Ensure that overland water flows are to be managed and conveyed safely across the precinct within the roads, reserves and identified public open space areas.
Ensure that post-development run-off does not result in a net negative impact on surrounding properties or the environment, resulting in damage to public and private assets.
Ensure that sustainable water management practices are applied, where practicable.
Acknowledge and safely accommodate with design, the overland flow flooding and stormwater conveyance in residential and ground floor frontage treatments.

A Water Management Control Plan shall be submitted in accordance with the Principles, Objectives and Controls contained in this section and Section 8.2.6.7 – Water Management Control Plan. It must address:
For a building that is part of or adjacent to an overland flow path or flood storage area:
For a building that is not part of an overland flow path or flood storage area:

A piped drainage reticulation system capable of carrying the 5% AEP stormwater flows is to be provided throughout the precinct for all roads, public domain areas and private lots. This system must be designed and constructed to Council standards and specifications and reasonable satisfaction. This drainage infrastructure is to be dedicated to Council at appropriate stages in the development process for ongoing operation by Council.
Excess peak flows are to be detained in both on-site and collective detention systems.

Excess peak flows from private lots, public roads and public domain are to be detained in both on-site and collective detention systems. Detention systems are to be integrated into a sustainable overall water management plan for the site which may include WSUD and rainwater harvesting.

Peak flows are to be limited throughout the catchment in a 1% AEP storm event to estimated peak flows under 1999 conditions, regardless of whether future redevelopment within the catchment occurs which improves the quantity of overland flow entering the precinct.
Lower flows are to be managed within the landscape and directed through landscape water quality biotreatment systems (Water Sensitive Urban Design) including deep soil.
On-site detention (OSD) systems are to be integrated into a sustainable overall water management plan for the site, where possible.
Subject to maintaining environmental flows and irrigation of the public domain landscapes, rainwater must be captured and used on site wherever feasible.

rules.rule_title: The following principles apply to all development in Melrose Park:
rules.rule_text: The floor space is generally consistent with the Gross Floor Area (GFA) as derived from the Floor Space Ratio (FSR).

rules.rule_title: The following principles apply to all development in Melrose Park:
rules.rule_text: The street network and building locations shall be consistent with the Masterplan to be designed to enable deep soil planting, reinforce the human scale of the streets, and enable views to the sky in streets and public places.

rules.rule_title: The following principles apply to all development in Melrose Park:
rules.rule_text: Building depth, bulk, and separation protects amenity, daylight penetration, and privacy between adjoining developments, and minimises the negative impacts of buildings on the amenity of the public domain.

rules.rule_title: The following principles apply to all development in Melrose Park:
rules.rule_text: Buildings should align with the streets so that positive spaces are formed within the streets and the lots.

rules.rule_title: The following principles apply to all development in Melrose Park:
rules.rule_text: Towers are to be appropriately proportioned and maximise their slender form.

rules.rule_title: The following principles apply to all development in Melrose Park:
rules.rule_text: The design and materials selection of buildings and the public domain are to contribute to a high quality, durable, and sustainable urban environment.

rules.rule_title: The following principles apply to all development in Melrose Park:
rules.rule_text: Buildings are organised to create spatially defined streets and courtyards that are well proportioned, comfortable, safe, functional, and attractive.

rules.rule_title: The following principles apply to all development in Melrose Park:
rules.rule_text: The collective built form should reinforce the variety evidenced in the topography and the spatial organisation of the streets and open spaces.

rules.rule_title: The following principles apply to all development in Melrose Park:
rules.rule_text: Variety within the precinct is to be derived from the detailed resolution of the buildings and not from excessive differences in the form of the buildings and/or the selection of materials.
Regulate the density of development identifying a maximum GFA for lots, resulting from the maximum floor space ratio in the PLEP 2023.
Ensure development floor plate sizes and building footprints are not excessive.
The maximum GFA for any development lot is to be generally consistent with the GFA as shown in Figure 8.2.6.6.2.1 – Maximum Gross Floor Area.
The GFA attributed to each lot results from the FSR controls in the PLEP 2023 or as otherwise nominated in a Notice of Development Consent granted by a relevant consent authority.

The indicative allocation of the total floor space relates to the Masterplan and is based on the capacity of the building envelope on each lot. The GFA is calculated at 75% of the building envelopes and the Gross Building Area (GBA) for residential development.
The maximum GFA is approximate for each lot and includes all buildings accommodated on a development lot.

The floor space is to be generally distributed as shown in the setback, building height, and street hierarchy figures. The perimeter-block is to be retained and floor space is not to be redistributed into towers where heights would enable greater height.

Development applications must submit supporting plans that demonstrate the GFA outcome on the development lot is consistent with PLEP 2023 or as otherwise nominated in a Notice of Development Consent granted by a relevant consent authority.

Should a maximum GFA not be able to be achieved for a development lot, or where there are minor variations, that amount of GFA may potentially be transferred to another development lot under the same ownership subject to consideration against the relevant provisions in this DCP and maintaining the gross permitted FSR across the development lots.

rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.2 STREET, BLOCK, OPEN SPACE, AND BUILDING LAYOUT

rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.2 STREET, BLOCK, OPEN SPACE, AND BUILDING LAYOUT

rules.rule_text: Enable streets to be dedicated to Council.

rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.2 STREET, BLOCK, OPEN SPACE, AND BUILDING LAYOUT
rules.rule_text: Accommodate passive and active recreational needs of the residents and workers.

rules.section: 8.2.6.2.2 STREET, BLOCK, OPEN SPACE, AND BUILDING LAYOUT
rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.2 STREET, BLOCK, OPEN SPACE, AND BUILDING LAYOUT
rules.rule_text: Manage overland floodwater as well as local stormwater drainage, water sensitive urban design (WSUD), and ground water.

rules.section: 8.2.6.2.2 STREET, BLOCK, OPEN SPACE, AND BUILDING LAYOUT
rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.2 STREET, BLOCK, OPEN SPACE, AND BUILDING LAYOUT
rules.rule_text: Minimise non-permeable surfaces.

rules.section: 8.2.6.2.2 STREET, BLOCK, OPEN SPACE, AND BUILDING LAYOUT
rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.2 STREET, BLOCK, OPEN SPACE, AND BUILDING LAYOUT
rules.rule_text: Enable buildings to achieve setbacks, solar access, and separation requirements, optimise the amenity of the apartments, define the public domain and minimise perceived density.

rules.section: 8.2.6.2.2 STREET, BLOCK, OPEN SPACE, AND BUILDING LAYOUT
rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.2 STREET, BLOCK, OPEN SPACE, AND BUILDING LAYOUT
rules.rule_text: The street network, pedestrian connections, block layout, and location of buildings shall be generally consistent with Figure 8.2.6.6.1 – Masterplan, Figure 8.2.6.6.8.1 – Street Hierarchy, and Figure 8.2.6.6.9.1 – Public Domain Plan.

rules.section: 8.2.6.2.2 STREET, BLOCK, OPEN SPACE, AND BUILDING LAYOUT
rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.2 STREET, BLOCK, OPEN SPACE, AND BUILDING LAYOUT
rules.rule_text: All streets are to be at ground level and public streets are to be dedicated to Council.

rules.section: 8.2.6.2.2 STREET, BLOCK, OPEN SPACE, AND BUILDING LAYOUT
rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.2 STREET, BLOCK, OPEN SPACE, AND BUILDING LAYOUT
rules.rule_text: Pedestrian connections that are above basements and privately owned should be publicly accessible 24/7.
Provide a coherent spatial and built form structure for the precinct.
Create meaningful variety related to street character and topography.
Define the streets, intersections, and open spaces in plan and in section.
Enable the resolution of quality architecture within the building envelopes.
Optimise the number of units with outlook to open spaces, courtyards, and views.
Minimise overshadowing on open spaces and residential development.
Minimise perceived density.
Provide view corridors within the site and to the surrounding context.
Enable satisfactory resolution of the slope and the water management of the precinct.
The building envelopes as defined in Figure 8.2.6.6.1 – Masterplan are to form the basis of the architectural resolution.
All view corridors as defined by the streets and pedestrian connections in Figure 8.2.6.6.1 – Masterplan are to be retained.
The floor space is to be distributed as shown in Figure 8.2.6.6.2.1 – Maximum Gross Floor Area.

The perimeter block residential buildings up to 6 storeys must extend for the full frontage of lots within the nominated street setbacks and except where there are courtyards of pedestrian connections.
The maximum length of all residential buildings 10 storeys and above must be no greater than 50 metres.
Reinforce the appropriate spatial definition of streets and public spaces.
Emphasise the importance of the street as a distinct spatial entity and design the street interface and street wall with an appropriate human scale and sense of enclosure for the street.
Ensure consistent street frontages with buildings having common setbacks and alignments.
Provide building forms that achieve comfortable public domain conditions for pedestrians, with adequate daylight, appropriate scale, and adequate mitigation of wind effects on tower buildings.
Create a clear delineation between public and private space.
Provide a landscape interface between residential buildings and streets, with room for street trees.
Emphasise the courtyard spaces as a distinct spatial entity and design with an appropriate human scale and sense of enclosure and landscaping.
Reinforce important elements of the local context including public spaces, key intersections, public and heritage buildings, and landscape elements.
Provide space on residential sites for ground level residents to engage appropriately with the street and for landscape that contributes to the public domain.
Building setbacks from the streets should comply with those shown in Figure 8.2.6.6.6.1 – Street Setbacks.

A 400mm articulation zone is permitted forward of the setback, in which building elements such as bay windows, balconies, and shading devices may occupy a maximum of approximately one third of the area of the façade. Services or lift shafts are not permitted in the articulation zone.
Setbacks should be measured perpendicular to the boundary to the outer faces of the building. Elements in the articulation zone are excluded.
Above the 4-6 storey component and the Town Centre podium, buildings are to be set back as shown in Section 8.2.2.6.10 – Indicative Application of Building Envelopes.

The ground floor, first and second floors of the NSR 2 and EWR 4 intersection and the NSR 3 and EWR 4 intersection may extent into the front setback a maximum of 3 metres measured form the front building line. Refer to Figure 8.2.6.2.4.1 – Street Wall Heights at Key Intersection. In plan the decreased street setback can extend for a distance of up to 25 metres along each street. Refer to Figure 8.2.6.2.4.2 – Indicative Corner Activation at Key Intersections, Plan (NSR 2 and EWR 4 Highest Priority and EWR 4 Second Highest Priority).

All buildings with the decreased setback at the NSR 2 and EWR 4 intersection and the NSR 3 and EWR 4 intersection are to relate to each other and define the space of the intersection up to a height of 3 levels. The corner design is to incorporate a building form such as a splayed setback, orthogonal recess to address the intersection. The levels above the ground floor are to relate to the ground floor resolution.

Setbacks should be measured perpendicular to the boundary to the outer faces of the building. Refer to Figure 8.2.6.2.4.3 – Street Wall Height in Town Centre. Elements (such as awnings and signage) in the articulation zone are excluded.
The separation distances of buildings across courtyards are 24 metres minimum building to building and is to be appropriately landscaped.

The separation distances of buildings across any pedestrian connections shall be 12 metres building to building. Within this space, a straight pedestrian path of a minimum 4 metres in width is to be located. Private gardens and entrances to apartments are permitted from these pedestrian paths. Refer to Figure 8.2.6.2.5.1 – Pedestrian Connections.
Issues of visual and noise privacy are to be addressed in the design of the buildings.
Separation distances should be measured perpendicular to the boundary to the outer faces of the building. Elements in the articulation zone are excluded.

The maximum floorplate for a residential tower over 8 storeys should be 1,000m2(the floorplate shall be measured to the outside face of the building inclusive of balconies, vertical and horizontal circulation, internal voids, and external walls).

The maximum length of the part of a building above 8 storeys should be 50m. In Melrose Park South the tower component height should be approximately double the height of the podium component (e.g. an 18-storey building where 12-storey minimum tower sits on 6-storey maximum podium).
Tower forms should not extend around corners so that they are ‘L’ shaped in plan.
Upper levels of towers should not extend over the lower levels and create under-croft spaces.
The higher building forms are to be integrated with the lower levels and should define positive spaces for streets, open spaces, and courtyards.
Towers should meet sustainability measures.
Tower design should respond to context, climate, and views.
Minimum floor to floor heights shall be provided in accordance with Table 8.2.6.2.8.1 – Minimum floor to floor heights. Table 8.2.6.2.8.1 – Minimum floor to floor heights

rules.section: 8.2.6.2.9 THE PERIMETER BLOCK BUILDINGS AND PODIUM
rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.9 THE PERIMETER BLOCK BUILDINGS AND PODIUM
rules.rule_text: Define the space of the street, pedestrian connections, parks, and courtyards by articulating their edges with perimeter-block buildings and podiums.

rules.section: 8.2.6.2.9 THE PERIMETER BLOCK BUILDINGS AND PODIUM
rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.9 THE PERIMETER BLOCK BUILDINGS AND PODIUM
rules.rule_text: Create visual interest and variety in the streetscape within an overall framework of consistency in the definition of the street and its character.

rules.section: 8.2.6.2.9 THE PERIMETER BLOCK BUILDINGS AND PODIUM
rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.9 THE PERIMETER BLOCK BUILDINGS AND PODIUM
rules.rule_text: Reveal the topography and provide rhythm.

rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.9 THE PERIMETER BLOCK BUILDINGS AND PODIUM
rules.rule_text: Provide a façade design that enhances the walking experience.

rules.section: 8.2.6.2.9 THE PERIMETER BLOCK BUILDINGS AND PODIUM
rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.9 THE PERIMETER BLOCK BUILDINGS AND PODIUM
rules.rule_text: Optimise active frontages in the Town Centre and at key intersections.

rules.section: 8.2.6.2.9 THE PERIMETER BLOCK BUILDINGS AND PODIUM
rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.9 THE PERIMETER BLOCK BUILDINGS AND PODIUM
rules.rule_text: The perimeter-block buildings and the podiums should:

rules.section: 8.2.6.2.9 THE PERIMETER BLOCK BUILDINGS AND PODIUM
rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.9 THE PERIMETER BLOCK BUILDINGS AND PODIUM
rules.rule_text: Under-crofts or other interruptions of the street wall that expose the underside of towers and amplify their presence on the street are not encouraged.

rules.section: 8.2.6.2.9 THE PERIMETER BLOCK BUILDINGS AND PODIUM
rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.9 THE PERIMETER BLOCK BUILDINGS AND PODIUM
rules.rule_text: All Development Applications should include a streetscape analysis and provide details of the street wall and perimeter-block. Submissions should include:

rules.section: 8.2.6.2.9 THE PERIMETER BLOCK BUILDINGS AND PODIUM
rules.rule_group: 8.2.6.2.9 THE PERIMETER BLOCK BUILDINGS AND PODIUM
rules.rule_text: Above ground car parking is only permitted for 3 levels in the Town Centre. It is to be sleeved by other uses on the East/West frontages EWR 6 and Hope Street. On the North/South frontages, it is to be screened.
Ground floor commercial uses should be located to activate the public domain.
Service frontages should be minimised.
The internal tenancy widths, foyers, and lobbies should create a fine grain frontage.
Ground floor commercial uses should include:
An appropriate freeboard at ground floor level is to be provided, where required.
Fire escapes and service doors should be designed to complement the commercial frontage and be seamlessly incorporated into the façade with quality materials.
Colonnades are not encouraged.
All required major services should be incorporated in the design of the ground floor frontage at Development Application stage, refer to Section 8.2.6.2.21 – Servicing and Utilities.
Security doors or grilles should be designed to be fitted internally behind the shopfront, fully retractable and a minimum 50% transparent when closed.
Deliver a ground floor that achieves amenity and privacy for residents as well as engagement with and passive surveillance of the street.
Enable a landscape setting where buildings are set back from the public domain.
Provide appropriate amenity for all residential apartments, including:
Locate the disability access so that it relates seamlessly to the building design.
Minimise the impact of basements.
Basements are to be located under the footprints of the buildings. They can extend under courtyards but not into the street setbacks, refer to Figure 8.2.6.2.11.2 – Apartment below street level.

Generally, ground floor apartment levels should be a minimum of 500mm and maximum of 1500mm above the adjacent footpath level except where the buildings front the pedestrian connections or additional height above the ground is required for privacy and/or to address the slope. Refer to Figure 8.2.6.2.11.1 – Residential ground floor.

Where apartment have individual entries from the street, a front door with a distinct entry space within the apartment should be provided. Individual apartment entries should be understated, with post boxes and street numbers located at the common entry. Individual entries are permitted from the pedestrian connections.
Unless easy ramp access can be provided without compromising the entrance to the building or the ground floor apartments, disability access should be provided as per AS 1428.
Apartments cannot be located below the street level except in the following situations at Council’s discretion (refer to Figure 8.2.6.2.11.1 – Residential ground floor):
The head height of the windows is not to be more than 300mm from the underside of the slab above.
The ground floor design including variations to floor levels is to:
The setback area should be designed to relate to the footpath and as common property for landscaping.

Canopy trees can be provided within setbacks that are 5 metres or greater, contributing to the landscape character of the street and residential amenity. Canopy trees should be planted in this area, a minimum 3.5 metres from any structure. Trees are to achieve greater than 13 metres mature height and spread, at the rate of 1 canopy tree for every 15 lineal metres of frontage.
Establish lower scale planting including hedges at street boundary for a minimum of 1 metre in street setback zone.
Establish canopy planting in courtyards to achieve amenity and privacy for residents as well as contributing to the street.
Co-locate the deep soil planting with the courtyard planting where the courtyards face the street setback.
Minimise impervious surfaces at ground level in the setback areas.
All required major services should be incorporated in the design of the ground floor frontage at Development Application stage, refer to Section 8.2.6.2.21 – Servicing and Utilities.
A fully illustrated and coordinated ground floor design, showing all the necessary levels and detail, should accompany applications. Drawings should include the following:
Upper levels of buildings should not extend over the lower levels.
Building floor plates and sections should define positive spaces for streets, open spaces, and courtyards.
Building indentations providing light and ventilation to apartments should have a minimum width to depth ratio of 2:1.
High-level windows should not be used as the primary source of light and ventilation for habitable rooms.
Where practicable, balconies should be rectangular in shape with the longer side parallel to the façade of the building.
Divisions between apartment balconies should be of solid construction and extend from floor to ceiling.
Common open space should include a unisex WC, seating, solid sun shading, and a BBQ and food preparation area with a sink.

Balustrades should take account of sightlines to balance the need for privacy within apartments and views out of apartments. A proportion of solid or translucent material should be used, which will vary according to outlook and height relationships.
The following details should be resolved in principle and shown on drawings at Development Application stage so as not to compromise amenity, built form, and aesthetics at a later stage:
Apartment design should consider incorporating suitable spaces that can be utilised as a work from home space.
Improve amenity of balconies in high-rise apartments above 8 storeys and apartments fronting noisy environments.
Provide acoustic attenuation for internal living areas.
Improve thermal environment.
Balance ventilation and wind impacts in high-rise apartment balconies.
Maximise daylight access, views, and comfort of balconies.
Wintergardens are only permitted above 8 storeys or where there are negative external impacts such as high levels of noise.
Wintergardens should:

All wintergardens are to have a balustrade less than 1.4m above finished floor level and a contiguous and permanently openable area between the balustrade and the ceiling level of not less than 25% of this area. This restriction shall apply to all elevations if the wintergarden has multiple elevations.
A generous opening should be provided between the wintergarden and any adjacent living area to allow connection of the spaces when ambient conditions are suitable.
Acoustic control for living areas and bedrooms should be provided on the internal façade line between the wintergarden and the living area or bedroom.

Glazing in the external façades of a wintergarden should have a solar absorption of less than 10% glass so as not to have solar heat absorption greater than what a clear float glass might be of the same composition.
The flooring of the wintergarden should be an impervious finish and provide exposed thermal mass.
Air conditioning units should not be located on wintergarden balconies.
Wintergarden areas able to be excluded from Gross Floor Area should be limited to a depth of 3 metres.
Enhance the: a) Amenity of the balcony and interior spaces. b) Design of the building façades.
Provide:
Ensure that the design of climate control devices can: a) Provide optimum control. b) Be easily cleaned. c) Assist in providing both visual and acoustic privacy.
The dwelling mix identified in Table 8.2.6.2.16.1 – Dwelling Mix is to be used as a guide for the apartments in Melrose Park: Table 8.2.6.2.16.1 – Dwelling Mix

A maximum 25% of the total apartments can be split into a pair of dual key apartments (which are considered to be one apartment for the purpose of dwelling mix). In all combinations the size and amenity of each dual key apartment should be consistent with the ADG.
Dual key apartments are to be under one strata title.
Consider the provision of apartment designs in sole occupancy units that are fully serviced but that have internal moveable walls, subject to compliance with the Building Code of Australia (BCA).

Ensure that materials contribute to the coherence of the precinct so that one building does not stand out from another. Variety within the precinct is derived from the detail resolution of the buildings and not from excessive differences in the selection of materials.
Use materials that meet sustainability objectives and requirements.
Select a palette of materials for the buildings that enable a complementary response with the finishes in public domain.
Employ materials that are durable, of an appropriate scale, and easily maintained.
A selected palette of materials for buildings, fencing, and retaining walls are to be agreed in consultation with Council.

Materials should: a) Ensure that one building does not stand out one from another building. b) Meet sustainability requirements of embodied energy. c) Be durable, of an appropriate scale, and easily maintained. d) Complement the materials in the public domain.
Relate to the scale and materiality of the buildings.
Define the public/private edge.
Provide privacy and visibility.
Be durable.
Relate to and reveal the slope of the land.
Fencing is to:
Fencing to private terraces where ground floor units extend into the street setback are to be designed to relate to any fencing on the property boundary.
The height of fences can vary but be no greater than 2 metres.
Reinforce the built form and open space structure of the precinct.
Expand and enhance the public domain.
Provide outlook from the apartments.
Provide a communal space for relaxation and communal activities.
Provide passive surveillance opportunities in public areas.
Have generous planting.
Assist with reducing urban heat.
Assist with flood management.
Provide visual separation between buildings.
Courtyards are to be located as shown in Figure 8.2.6.6.3.1 – Courtyard Locations and have a minimum width (east – west) of 24 metres.
Courtyards should:
Courtyard levels are to be designed to appropriately address flood risk management.
Where courtyards are located over basements, canopy planting is to be set down in the slab.
Courtyards are to be designed to respond to the street interface (Refer to Figure 8.2.6.2.20.1 – Courtyard Basement – Interface with Street).
Link the external spatial network with the internal spatial network in the Town Centre.
Improve connectivity.
Encourage walkability.
Maintain the number of safe routes of travel throughout Melrose Park North as shown in the Masterplan.
Provide direct access and sight lines from the 6 metre north/south pedestrian path from the EWR2 to the Parramatta River through the Town Centre.
Define the access internally to reflect the external space.
Allow for pedestrian access to Hope Street during the hours of operation of the Light Rail through the Town Centre.
