Ensure that the resulting population density is appropriate for the characteristics of the site, its immediate surrounds and LGA.
Encourage an overall built form and building layout which respond appropriately to the principles detailed in the overall objectives of this site specific DCP.
The following areas may be included as part of the site area for the purposes of calculating FSR:
Floor space ratios must be in accordance with the FSR LEP map reproduced at Figure 8.5.7.4.2.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Enhance the existing natural features of the site including topography, geology; vegetation/vegetation communities; micro climate; hydrology (surface and sub-surface).

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Enhance the natural environmental performance of the site by coordinating water and soil management, solar access, micro-climate, tree canopy and habitat values.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Retain existing trees where possible and use landscaping to make a positive contribution to the streetscape and neighbourhood.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Provide water sensitive urban design for the management of stormwater drainage.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Ese open space areas, new roads and pedestrian links to assist with stormwater management, provide deep soil zones and maximise rainfall infiltration.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Design all public spaces and landscaping to a high-quality with a demonstrated consistent design.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Retain, protect and enhance areas identified as having high or moderate ecological significance area.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Site coverage should provide for adequate deep soil, communal spaces, streets and separation between buildings.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Existing high ecological significance trees must be retained where possible.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: The setback to Pennant Hills Road must be densely landscaped with species endemic to the area. This setback shall be provided as a deep soil zone with no basement or sub-floor structures.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Landscaping must use predominantly indigenous species that reflect the region’s character of the Sydney Blue Gum High Forest and Sydney Turpentine-Ironbark Forest vegetation communities. Opportunities to plant species representative of the communities and the existing areas of moderate and high ecological significance located on the site are to be explored provided planting of these species does not present a danger to residents and the public.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Selected plant species must provide form, enclosure, texture and colour. The planting should also take on a further role in providing biodiversity, shade and protection.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: A mix of local trees, shrubs and grasses must be used to create attractive, colourful and low maintenance landscaped areas.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: All building setbacks are to be landscaped.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Any Development Application must include a detailed landscape plan and landscape design report prepared by a qualified landscape architect. The landscape plans are to include details of plant species, pot sizes, mature height, tree protection measures and a detailed maintenance program.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Deep soil zones must be provided for the first 3 metres of all property boundaries other than Pennant Hills Road which requires a 6 metre deep soil zone (Refer Control C.03).

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Landscaping and buildings should operate as an integrated and sustainable system, resulting in greater aesthetic quality and amenity for both future and existing, surrounding residents and the adjoining public domain.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Landscape design should optimise usability, privacy and social opportunity, equitable access and respect for neighbours’ amenity, and provide for practical establishment and long-term management.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Landscaping should enhance the existing natural attributes of the site (including existing vegetation and topography) and seek to maintain and enhance those features as far as possible.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Deep landscaped setbacks should be provided to Pennant Hills Road to enhance amenity along this frontage.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Street trees and landscaping should be provided along footpaths to enhance the quality of the streetscape and maximise pedestrian amenity.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Tree and plant species endemic to the area should be used. Communal Open Space Areas

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: All communal open space areas must include the following:

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Communal Open Space on both Site A and Site B is to reflect the rectangular shape and approximate area size illustrated in the Public Domain Plan Figure at 8.5.7.2.1.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Communal open space areas should be sized to allow opportunities for passive and active recreation.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Well-defined paths should be provided to allow access to Pennant Hills Road and other public domain areas.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: A safe pedestrian environment should be provided.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Pedestrian links should be designed and located to assist in in providing increased casual surveillance.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Post-development peak flows from the development site must not exceed pre- development peak flows.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Landscape works must be undertaken in collaboration with the hydraulic and civil works to develop an integrated stormwater design.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Open space and green links should be provided to assist with stormwater management, provide deep soil zones and maximise rainfall infiltration.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: All development including landscape designs must incorporate WSUD measures including rain gardens, bioswales, biosinks, and water polishing ponds, wetlands and other constructed ecologies which can detain, retain and reuse water.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_title: Areas of High and Moderate Ecological Significance
rules.rule_text: Areas identified as being of high or moderate ecological significance are shown on Figure 8.5.7.5.1.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_title: Areas of High and Moderate Ecological Significance
rules.rule_text: Any development on land containing or immediately adjoining areas of high or moderate ecological significance must confirm the boundaries of the area of ecological significance with detailed analysis to ensure no adverse impacts to those areas occurs as a result of the development.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_title: Areas of High and Moderate Ecological Significance
rules.rule_text: A flora and fauna assessment must be submitted with any Development Application on land identified as containing areas of high or moderate ecological significance.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_title: Areas of High and Moderate Ecological Significance
rules.rule_text: Setbacks and siting of buildings must provide areas for deep soil/permeable surfaces, communal open space areas and private open spaces.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_title: Areas of High and Moderate Ecological Significance
rules.rule_text: The massing and siting of the buildings must:

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_title: Areas of High and Moderate Ecological Significance
rules.rule_text: Sites must be a minimum of 1,500m² for development of apartment buildings of 3 or more storeys.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_title: Areas of High and Moderate Ecological Significance
rules.rule_text: Sites must have a minimum frontage of 24 metres for development of apartment buildings of 3 or more storeys.

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: A detailed site analysis plan must be submitted with a Development Application proposing residential apartment building(s) and/or multi-unit residential development. The massing and siting of the buildings should:

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Buildings must be designed to:

rules.section: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_group: 8.5.7.6 LANDSCAPED SPACES AND AREAS OF ECOLOGICAL VALUE
rules.rule_text: Attached housing must demonstrate that the design principles of the Design Guide for Low Rise Housing Diversity and the SEPP (Housing Code) have been considered.
Ensure appropriate placement of development on the site to minimise impact to the creek line, reserve and neighbouring properties.
Ensure appropriate distribution of built form across the site to maximise view corridors to the reserve from James Ruse Drive.
Minimise adverse impacts from the proximity of James Ruse Drive on future apartments within the development.
Achieve a landscape transition that responds to the natural features and topography of the site.
Respond to the role of this site as a Target Site for greater residential density.
Ensure that development on the subject site complements without challenging the role of Target Site 7 as a gateway site.
Provide recognition through the allowable height of the visual prominence of the site for drivers on James Ruse Drive.
Ensure that the new development does provide a high-quality address to James Ruse Drive from the east and to North Rocks Road.

Ensure the new development provides a built form buffer for the creek corridor and development to the north from the noise and visual impacts of James Ruse Drive whilst allowing generous north-south view corridors across the site.
Provide a dwelling density that reflects the target site status of the land.
Provide high density residential development in proximity to public transport.
Ensure that an appropriate level of development is provided on the site that does not dominate the adjoining Target Site 7 or the lower scale development along North Rocks Road.
Mitigate adverse impacts on neighbouring properties.
Mitigate acoustic impacts of James Ruse Drive.
Ensure new development is appropriately setback from North Rocks Road.
Encourage solar penetration and view corridors through the site
Safeguard and protect the required riparian corridor and enable regeneration of native landscape to the creek banks and corridor.
Provide sufficient setback form the riparian corridor toa low private open space to occur at ground level.
Provide an apartment design that achieves a functional layout and high level of amenity.
Ensure that appropriate storage and facilities are provided within the unit.
Maximise opportunities for cross ventilation and solar access.
Ensure a direct relationship between living spaces and private open spaces for each dwelling.
Future development to be located generally in accordance with Figure 8.5.8.5.1.
Building depth is to be a maximum of 18 metres from glass line to glass line excluding balconies. Height of buildings
Building heights are not to exceed the maximum number of storeys shown in Figures 8.5.8.1.1 and 8.5.8.1.2.
Mezzanine levels will be counted as storeys.
Number of storeys excludes where basements protrude above ground due to topography on the site.
Additional height for architectural roof features is considered appropriate to achieve architectural excellence
No more than 150 dwellings may be provided on the site.

Average building setbacks and minimum separation distances between the built forms are to be provided in accordance with Figure 8.5.8.3.1 providing that any windows on the side facades of the buildings (excluding the eastern façade of Building E) are either frosted or high level to ensure adequate privacy is achieved.
The minimum front setback is to align with the predominant setback within the street or 9 metres whichever is greater.
The setback to buildings from James Ruse Drive is to be a minimum of 19 metres including balconies and access corridors.
The setback to the north eastern boundary is to be a minimum of 21 metres.
The setback to Russell Walker Reserve is to be a minimum of 8 metres.

Additional setbacks are to be provided for built form beyond the riparian corridor to the north to allow for generous private open space terraces and additional landscaping between the development and the riparian zone.
Plant growth is to be optimised by:
Planters are to be designed to support the appropriate soil depth and plant selection by:
Minimum soil depths are to accordance with the following: Table 8.5.8.3.1 – Minimum soil depths
Long continuous corridors servicing units are to be avoided.
All living areas and private open space areas are to be oriented to the north towards the creek corridor or Russell Walker Reserve.
Minimise the number of habitable rooms located to the south adjacent to James Ruse Drive.
Locate wet areas, service rooms and circulation to the southern portion of the buildings or adjacent to James Ruse Drive and North Rocks Road.
Provide the following minimum storage area to each unit:
A minimum of 50% of the storage requirement is to be provided within the unit via separate linen and storage cupboards and is not to include wardrobes or kitchen cupboards in the calculation.
Orient the development to maximise solar access to living areas and open spaces.
Ensure adjacent dwellings and their private open spaces achieve a reasonable level of solar access.
Provide adequate passive shading to north, west and east facing windows and private open space areas to minimise the need for reliance on fossil fuels for cooling in summer.
Minimise the number of south facing units and single aspect apartments
Buildings are to be oriented so that solar access to living areas and private open spaces is optimised.
Dual aspect apartments are to be maximised with no apartments to be south facing as their only orientation.
Living rooms and private open spaces for at least 70% of apartments in the development are to receive a minimum of three hours direct sunlight between 9:00am and 3:00pm in mid-winter.
A minimum of 60% of the communal open space areas must receive at least three hours of sunlight between 9:00am and 3:00pm on 21 June.
Buildings are to be designed to provide passive shading and glare control, especially in summer, by:
New development is to provide a high-quality architectural response to the site in terms of:
Elevation treatment is to be generally consistent with Figure 8.5.8.5.3.
Achieve legible way-finding through the site and convenient access into building for residents and visitors.
Ensure access to building entries is direct and clearly visible and a strong sense of address is provided to North Rocks Road.
Provide a range of pedestrian routes through the development to the creek corridor and opportunities to link the site to Russell Walker Reserve.
Minimise the visual impact of vehicle access areas to pedestrian areas, North Rocks Road and the development as a whole.
Achieve a single access point for vehicles from North Rocks Road, if possible.
Ensure vehicle access meets with sound traffic management principles.
Ensure that vehicle movement occurs in a safe and efficient manner.
Ensure that access for waste collection vehicles meets Council’s waste collection requirements. Car parking
Ensure that all car-parking demands generated by the development are accommodated on the development site.
Protect the free flow of traffic into and out of residential flat building developments and the surrounding street network.
Pedestrian links and entry points are to be provided generally in accordance with Figure 8.5.8.5.5 Pedestrian access.
Resident pedestrian paths through the site are to be located outside the core riparian corridor and protected waters.
A new public pathway is to be provided within the riparian corridor as an elevated walkway connecting the existing reserve to the new road access for Target Site 7.
Access points for pedestrians are to be separated from vehicle access driveways to ensure safety.
Pathway locations must ensure natural surveillance from primary living areas of adjoining units.
Design buildings to the north eastern boundary of the site to overlook the adjacent pedestrian pathway. A visually permeable, low fence to allow dwellings to address the pathway is preferred.
Bicycle lock-up facilities are to be provided adjacent to the main entries of the buildings and bicycle storage is to be provided as part of the parking space for each unit.
Building entrances are to be fully accessible. Entries are to be clearly visible to public areas and are to be located either directly from North Rocks Road or the access street to Target Site 7.
Identification signage is to be located at the entry to the site along North Rocks Road displaying clearly the property’s name and address.
Vehicle entry points are to be provided generally in accordance with Figure 8.5.8.6.1.
The preferred location for vehicle and service access is by the new access road for Target Site 7 along the site’s southern boundary avoiding multiple entry points along North Rocks Road.
The design and configuration of driveways shall be in accordance with Part 6 – Traffic and Transport of this DCP.
Potential pedestrian/vehicle conflicts are to be minimised by:
All vehicles, including waste and removalist trucks must be able to manoeuvre on-site without relying on access to Target Site 7.

The Roads and Maritime Services will be consulted in relation to any Development Application on the site. Road and traffic management improvements to North Rocks Road are to be undertaken as specified by and to the satisfaction of the Roads and Maritime Services and Council.
Car parking is to be provided in the locations shown in Figure 8.5.8.6.2.
Car parking shall be in accordance with Part 6 – Traffic and Transport of this DCP.

Car parking is to be located within the basement of any new development. The line of the basement car park shall fit generally within the building footprint as shown in Figure 8.5.8.6.2 with considerations given to optimising consolidated areas of deep soil.
The basement car park shall be designed to mitigate flood impacts.
No car parking is to be exposed to the creek riparian corridor or to the reserve.
Any car parking above natural ground due to the slope of the land is to be sleeved behind residential uses or landscaped terraces.
Maintain and enhance the natural vegetation along Darling Mills Creek and Russell Walker Reserve.
Maximise landscaping and provide opportunities for the planting of native species.
Provide high-quality landscaping along the Target Site 7 access road and frontage to North Rocks Road.
Provide a high-quality landscaped outlook and private open space for residents.
Retain the existing tree planting within the site where possible.
Minimise visual privacy impacts to the dwellings to the north east.
Maximise deep soil zones within the site.
Minimise the visual impact of the existing retaining wall to James Ruse Drive.
Provide high-quality communal open space for residents including a range of recreational opportunities for residents
Maximise opportunities for deep soil particularly adjacent to Darling Mills Creek, James Ruse Drive and North Rocks Road to contribute to the landscape character of these areas.
Assist with the management of water quality and the water table on the site.
Improve the amenity of the site and its surroundings by retaining existing trees or replacing them with the same or similar species
Contribute to the quality and amenity of communal open space over car parking areas.
Landscaping is to be provided in the locations shown in Figure 8.5.8.7.1.
Additional street trees of an appropriate species are to be provided along the North Rocks Road boundary.
Landscaped area is to comprise a minimum of 60% of the site area.
Areas less than 2 metres in width will be excluded from the landscape calculation.
A minimum of 2,300m² of common open space is to be provided.
The new access street to Target Site 7 is to be landscaped with semi mature street trees and landscaped frontages to reduce the visual impact of James Ruse Drive.
Consultation is required with Caltex to consider landscaping opportunities on and adjacent to the Hunter Pipeline easement prior to the lodgement of any Development Application.

A Vegetated Riparian Zone is to be provided along Darling Mills Creek in accordance with NSW Office of Water requirements (See Figure 8.5.8.7.4). It is to function as an ecological system and therefore cannot contain any access routes or recreational areas unless detailed as part of a Development Application for the land prior to issue of the Controlled Activity Approval.
Existing trees and vegetation in the riparian corridor and in the 1 in 100 year flood zone (refer to Flood Impact Report by HKMA dated 15/12/2011) are to be retained and the corridor re-
Erosion and sediment control works and water diversion structures are to be provided in accordance with the NSW Office of Water requirements.

A vegetation management plan must be prepared for approval by the NSW Office of Water as part of any development. It is to follow the Office of Water’s Guidelines for Vegetation Management Plans on Waterfront Land.
Endemic riparian species that overhang the creek should also be used and emergent aquatic vegetation restored where possible.
Existing trees on-site are to be retained. If it is not possible to retain existing trees then a replacement mature specimen of the same or similar species is to be provided.
Open spaces are to be concentrated on the northern side of the development.
Landscaping shall be designed to positively contribute to the site’s existing characteristics by:
The energy and solar efficiency of dwellings and the micro-climate of private open spaces are to be improved by:
Landscape design is to contribute to water and stormwater efficiency by:
Private and common spaces are to be clearly defined through landscape.
Communal open space is to be provided to the northern area of the site adjacent to the creek corridor and existing reserve.
The design of this area is to ensure privacy for ground floor units adjacent to it.
Communal open space is to be accessible to all residents.
Provision of a gym or pool must be restricted to indoors to minimise noise impacts to adjoining residents.
Deep soil is to be provided generally in the locations shown in Figures 8.5.8.6.2 and 8.5.8.6.3.
Deep soil is to comprise a minimum of 30% of the landscape area. (Note: the landscape area excludes the riparian corridor zone and the riparian recovery zone).
Minimise acoustic impacts from James Ruse Drive and North Rocks Road.
Minimise noise transmission in between dwellings and from common open areas.
Avoid overlooking of living areas and private open space.
Maximise opportunities for passive visual surveillance.
Stack ventilation should be considered to achieve cross ventilation in apartments where acoustic requirements will not allow operable windows to the southern facade of the development.
Any development proposal is to be accompanied by a noise impact assessment (Acoustic Report) detailing typical noise levels within dwellings.

Minimise direct overlooking of living areas and private open space areas of dwellings both within and between dwellings on-site and adjoining sites through building location and orientation, landscape, screening devices and window size, location and glass treatment.
Any plant and equipment for the development is to be screened and acoustically rted to avoid noise transference.
All waste storage and servicing will be accessible off the access road to Target Site 7 and comply with Council’s preferred waste management strategy.
A waste management plan shall be prepared for green and putrescible waste, garbage, glass, containers and paper.
Every dwelling will include a waste cupboard or temporary storage area of sufficient size to hold a single day’s waste and to enable source separation.
All waste storage will comply with Council’s Waste Management Information and Bin Bay Design Specifications for Multi-unit Developments

Development approval for the 27-33 North Rocks Road, North Rocks Target Site must have appropriate regard to the amenity of the adjoining residential development known as Target Site 7. Accordingly, any Development Application for the residential development of Lot 2 DP 1158967 shall have regard to the access requirements for development of the site.
Staging should be organised to follow a logical sequence and minimise disruption to surrounding development.
Be compatible with the surrounding heights of the adjacent mixed height single dwellings and two storey multi dwelling housing.
Explore built form that minimises any negative impacts on the Russell Street streetscape/landscape.

Explore a range of building forms that represent a transition between the low scale residential forms to the east and the two storey multi dwelling housing developments to the west and higher building forms within the site.
Preserve the existing views of the historic school precinct from Windsor Road. Heritage
Reflect and respect the significant heritage buildings and identified curtilage. Land Use and Density
Achieve an appropriate relationship to the topography of the site and ensure the built form does not adversely impact on the solar access and privacy of adjoining owners.
Provide adequate communal open space.
Respect the historic low density visual setting of the historic school curtilage. Access
Provide comfortable and safe pedestrian access for future residents.
Not adversely impact on the residential ambiance of Russell Street.
Limit vehicular access into and out of site to three locations along Russell Street. Urban Structure
Provide an appropriate transition between the low-rise, low-density housing skirting the east and west boundaries and higher density residential development within the site.
Where possible retain and protect any existing mature trees within the site that have been identified for retention.
Provide appropriate communal streetscape character of Russell Street with sympathetic landscaping treatment.
Have regard to the physical setting of the site, including the fall from the north-east to the north- west.
Ensure the retention and ongoing maintenance of the identified historic plantings associated with the school through the provision of generous space for spread and protection of root zones.

Retention/reinforcement of perimeter landscape. Augmentation of internal landscape to reinforce and reinstate the gardenesque characteristics of both the site and the area. This will also provide the basis for a series of active passive communal recreational places within the site.
Adaptive re-use of the heritage buildings and curtilage for communal active functions.
10 metre building setback from historic school buildings.
Low rise development (to maximum two storeys plus attic) within the zone contiguous with curtilage and heritage buildings and generally paralleling Windsor Road.
Primary vehicular access to the site from Russell Street located to take advantage of site slopes and thus giving vehicular entry to basements associated with individual building complexes.
Pedestrian entry zones identified to provide for a range of alternate access points from both Windsor Road and Russell Street.
Three storeys plus attic buildings located behind perimeter landscaping to Russell Street. Facades to be articulated.
Six storey residential flat building located in the centre of the site with a norther aspect. Siting designed to maximise panoramic views and minimise external impacts.
Maximum development capacity of 130 dwellings consistent with the recommendations in the Traffic Report.
Internal roads and vehicular access/visitor parking to be lightly incised into the site.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: Achieve coherent site planning and development that relates to the natural contours of the site and contributes to the character of the area.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: Protect, contribute and enhance the existing residential character and amenity. Setbacks

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: Provide setbacks that compliment the surrounding setting and allow flexibility.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: Front setback to be provided to enhance the existing character and streetscape quality of Russell street and provide opportunity for visually significant as well as functional landscape.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: Ensure that the scale and bulk of new buildings have regard to the natural topography and retain vegetation within the site.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: Ensure that new buildings are compatible in bulk and scale with the surrounding developments.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: Ensure that the new buildings have minimum impact on the neighbouring properties in terms of overshadowing, privacy and views.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: Ensure that new buildings within the two storey zone are compatible in height, bulk and scale with the remaining heritage buildings.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: Future development to be located generally in accordance with Figure 8.5.9.2.2.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: The site coverage shall be a maximum 50% of the site area. Setbacks

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: Setbacks are to complement the existing and future desired streetscape of the area.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: Setbacks are to provide sufficient area for landscaping to compliment building form.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: Front setbacks for the proposed development are to minimise negative impact on the existing landscape.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: Side and rear setbacks of the proposed development are to minimise any adverse impacts such as overshadowing and privacy between the proposed and existing developments.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: Development setbacks shall be in accordance with Figure 8.5.9.3.1. Table 8.5.9.3.1 - Setbacks

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: The height of new buildings shall be related to the topography of the site.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: The height of new building shall not exceed the bulk and scale of buildings on adjoining lands.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: Building height and bulk shall be located on the site to ensure that there is no significant loss of amenity to adjacent dwellings and the public domain.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: The design of the units adjacent to Russell Street properties should provide for articulation of built form and fenestration to provide visual interest and diversity.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: The maximum height of any two storey building and attic abutting the M2 Motorway, Windsor Road or Russell Street shall not exceed 7.2 metres (height to eaves).

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: The maximum height of any three storey building and attic or part of a building addressing Russell Street shall not exceed 12 metres to the eaves or 14 metres to the ridge.

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: The maximum height of any building abutting the M2 Motorway along the northern boundary shall not exceed more than six residential storeys (18 metres height to eaves or 20 metres maximum ridge height).

rules.section: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_group: 8.5.9.4 BUILT FORM, HEIGHT OF BUILDINGS AND SITE PLANNING
rules.rule_text: The attic level in any unit may contain a maximum of one bedroom (with associated wardrove and ensuite). The maximum total useable floor area permitted in the attic is 25m² (including the area of the ensuite and wardrobe). No additional floor space will be permitted in the attic area.
Landscaping is to be appropriately scaled and locate relative to both the building bulk, incorporating existing vegetation where possible.
The landscape area shall be a minimum 50% of the area of the site, exclusive of access driveways and parking.
Areas less than 2 metres in width will be excluded from the landscaped area calculation.
A minimum of 25% of the landscaping area shall permit deep soil planting.

Landscape plans shall clearly demonstrate that an additional quantum of mature landscaping will be provided and located in a form that reflects as loosely as possible the landscape that exists as at January 2000 (refer Figure 8.5.9.4.2).
A 7,500mm wide deep soil planting landscape medium island is to be provided at the entry to the central driveway off Russell Street which provides access to the six storey building basement.
Mature landscaping is to be provided on Russell Street to supplement existing trees and enhance screening of the future development from Russell Street
Provide sufficient and convenient on-site parking for residents and visitors and hence maintain the amenity of adjoining properties and the efficiency of the road network.
Ensure that vehicular access to and from the development is simple, safe and direct.
Car parking shall be located underground where practicable, to minimise the height of buildings above the natural ground level.
Driveway design shall provide safe and efficient ingress/egress to the site.
The design of driveways and parking areas shall minimise the visual impact of hard paved areas
The driveway design shall make provision for service vehicles where practicable. Public Roads
Line marking and curb treatment to delineate left and right turns for vehicles exiting Russell Street is to be provided to council’s requirements.
All car parking areas and spaces shall be designed in accordance with Part 6 – Traffic and Transport.
Tandem car parking may be considered depending upon the merits of the proposal having regard to overall car parking provision.
A carwash bay must be provided in accordance with the following:
Car parking design to be generally in accordance with Figure 8.5.9.5.1. Pedestrian Access
Separate pedestrian access shall be provided from the street independent of vehicular access.
Pedestrian access shall be legible, inviting, safe and provide visible interest.
Pedestrian access to be in accordance with Figure 8.5.9.2.2.

Given the existing width of Russell Street the opportunity to establish perpendicular to the kerb car parking zones for visitors, together with associated landscaping should be explored with the Council during the DA design phase. The introduced landscape should be no less than one mature street tree for every four car parking spaces.
Vehicular access to the site should reflect the principles shown in Figure 8.5.9.5.1.
The design and configuration of access ways and driveways shall be in accordance with Part 6 of this DCP.
Private open space areas and habitable rooms of proposed and adjacent existing dwellings shall be reasonably protected from overlooking by considering, but not being limited to:
Private open space areas and habitable rooms shall be reasonably protected from uncomfortable levels of external noise by considering, but not being limited to:
Windows of living rooms with direct outlook to any living room window of any proposed, or and/or existing adjoining dwelling living rooms within 9 metres shall be:
Dividing walls and floors between dwellings shall be constructed to limit noise transmission to 45 STC (Sound Transmission Class) in accordance with Part F(5) of the Building Code of Australia.
Submission of an acoustic report prepared by a suitably qualified person that addresses internal noise levels of dwellings based on AS 3671 – Road Traffic Noise Intrusion Guidelines
Ensure reasonable access of sunlight to living areas within buildings and open space areas around buildings in winter and minimise the need for artificial heating.
Ensure adjacent open space/areas, living areas of adjacent dwellings, and communal areas are not deprived of reasonable solar access.
Minimise the need for artificial lighting in dwellings during the day.
Provide adequate shading to internal areas and private open space in summer to minimise the need for artificial cooling.
Sunlight is to be available to the majority of living areas and private and communal open space areas of the proposed dwellings, and to any adjoining dwellings having regard but not limited to:
Locate the private open space areas to achieve 4 hours sunlight between 9:00am and 3:00pm on 21 June.
A target of 70% of units to achieve solar access to living areas.
Private and communal open space areas to be located to receive adequate sunlight and shading, maintain privacy and minimise noise.
Each dwelling shall provide an area of useable private open space, or private courtyard area, which has direct private access from the dwelling.
Area(s) of communal open space shall be provided for the recreational needs of the future residents.
The location of all open space areas shall have regard to such requirements as solar access, outlook, noise minimisation, privacy and location of adjoining dwellings.
Ground floor dwellings shall be provided with courtyards.
Private open space areas shall be directly accessible form living areas of all dwellings.
For dwellings with ground level access private open space to be provided by way of courtyard shall be:
For Above Ground Level Dwellings
To provide for the recreational needs of the residents, communal open space is to be provided in the locations as shown in Figure 8.5.9.3.1.
Such open space area is to include equipment such as seats, shade structures, barbecues and children’s play equipment for passive recreational use.
Access to and through the common open space area shall be secured for use by residents of the development only.
The area provided shall be equivalent to the rate of 20m² per dwelling.
Provide site facilities that are adequate and conveniently located for fulfilling the resident’s needs.
Ensure that the site facilities are practical, attractive and easily maintained.

Rubbish and recycling bin enclosures, letter boxes, clothes drying areas and other site facilities should be adequate in size, made of durable, weatherproof materials, and to be visually integrated with the development. They need to be located having regard to the protection of residential amenity, vehicle serviced access, visual impact and residents access.

A minimum of 10m² of dedicated storage space shall be provided to each dwelling with a minimum clearance height of 2.1 metres from floor level. This can be provided in a way of an enclosure and as an extension of the dedicated car park for each unit.
An internal laundry shall be provided to each dwelling.
Letter boxes shall be provided in accordance with the delivery requirements of Australia Post.
A communal rubbish storage area shall be provided within the site. The storage area shall:
All dwellings shall be sited and designed to maximise natural cross-ventilation and solar access to all living area by:

Landscaping shall assist microclimate management by the strategic location of deciduous trees to permit winter sunlight access to living areas and provide summer shade to north exposed windows and other glass openings.
The building shall adopt:

The building shall achieve, as minimum, a 3.5 star rating by NatHERS in respect to energy efficiency and a greenhouse score of 4. Details of the rating are to be submitted with the Development Application. Elements include:
Retain the former school building, teacher’s residence and heritage curtilage within the development of the subject site for the benefit of future residents.
Enhance and preserve the fabric of the remaining heritage structures and provide for their long-
Future use of the former school building to provide a communal function for use by future residents of the subject site.
Future use of the former teacher’s residence as a caretaker’s residence and meeting room for the development.
The preservation and maintenance of historic school buildings and landscape plantings within the historic boundary of the former Baulkham Hills Public School.

The former school and teacher’s residence are to be used solely for the benefit of the residents of the subject development for uses such as a gymnasium, pool change room, caretaker’s residence and meeting room.
The historic buildings and curtilage are to be retained upon the same title as the subject site and maintained by the strata body corporate.
A Conservation Management Plan is to be prepared by a suitably qualified conservation architect and is to be submitted with a Development Application for the redevelopment of the subject site.

The historic buildings and curtilage are to be the subject of restoration works in accordance with a Conservation Management Plan and are to occur concurrent with the redevelopment of any part of the subject site and be completed prior to the issue of an occupation certificate and/or subdivision certificate.
Future development shall ensure the ongoing preservation and maintenance of the historic fig trees at the northern end of the historic school site.
The Plan shall have regard to the following documentation:

Compatibility of style and character of the proposed development with that of the predominant style and character of surrounding residential or heritage buildings shall be demonstrated within the Development Application.
Building materials and colours selected and utilised on the site are to be coordinated throughout the site and be compatible with the remaining heritage structures.
Provides a built form that relates strongly within the confines of the site and is sensitive to existing residential and educational land uses on surrounding sites.
Provides a built form that delivers a high-quality amenity outcome for residents, particularly to the west, where the development interfaces with Windsor Road.

Provides appropriate noise amelioration for residential uses to protect against existing noise in the surrounding precinct, particularly traffic noise generated from Windsor Road and James Ruse Drive.
Results in minimal overshadowing within the site, surrounding properties and public open spaces, to ensure that adequate levels of amenity are achieved throughout the year.
Achieves the desired orientation and organisation of the built form massing, noting that solar access requirements as per the Apartment Design Guide (ADG) may not be fully met.
Provides building separation that supports amenity and privacy, both within the development and to adjoining sites.
Provides communal and publicly accessible open space that incorporates opportunities for social gathering and passive recreation between buildings within the development.
Supports the predominant street pattern with buildings perpendicular to the lot, reinforcing the orthogonal grid in this location.
Prioritise the spatial definition of streets (public and private) and open spaces through the organisation of taller buildings, creating a continuation of the existing street wall.
Respond to and reinforce the existing urban form through a centrally located open space and minimising building height to the centre of site.
Ensure new development responds to the sloping topography, the context of surrounding development and the visual setting of the site between various residential buildings.
Ensure that new development responds to the constraints imposed by neighbouring sites and maximises positive visual outlook within the development and adjoining sites.
Ensure built form is organised in an orthogonal manner that supports the predominant subdivision pattern in this location.
Create a clear delineation between public, communal and private spaces.
Define and design the street alignment and setback area to achieve amenity and privacy for residents, as well as engagement with and passive surveillance of communal spaces.
Ensure the presentation of buildings to the internal streets provides clearly defined edges and corners, and an architectural resolution that relates to the ground plane with legible entries.
Maximum building heights shall be in accordance with Figure 8.5.10.2.2, utilising regular building forms that utilise the sloping topography and minimise the perceived density of development.

Height of new buildings are to ensure positive and cohesive relationships with other buildings, both on the site and off the site, and are to respond to the desired scale and character of the local area.
Floor to ceiling and floor to floor heights are to be in accordance with the NSW Apartment Design Guide.

Setbacks are to be measured perpendicular to the boundary to the outer faces of the building including balconies, winter gardens, screening and the like. A 1 metre articulation zone may be provided where primary private open spaces face communal open space.
Building setbacks are to be in accordance with Figures 8.5.10.2.3 to 8.5.10.2.6 and Table 8.5.10.2.1.
Minimum separation between buildings should be in accordance with Figure 8.5.10.2.7 and the NSW ADG requirements.
Habitable spaces are to be carefully positioned within each unit to ensure that visual and acoustic privacy is maximised.
Setbacks and separation must be measured perpendicular to the building face, inclusive of balconies, wintergardens, vertical and horizontal circulation, internal voids and external walls.
Communal open space and landscaped areas should be provided between buildings as shown in Figure 09 to promote opportunities for community interaction within the development.

Areas between buildings should allow for pedestrians to comfortably move between the buildings, and promote the principles of passive surveillance. These communal areas should provide a safe and unobstructed path of travel, as opposed to private space.
Communal open spaces are to be designed to maximise solar access in mid-winter and canopy cover in mid-summer.

Opportunities for seating and gathering spaces for passive recreation, play and informal activities such as outdoor dining are to be provided within the internal circulation system of the development is to be provided where appropriate.
Water Sensitive Urban Design principles shall be implemented in communal open space areas.

Fencing fronting the communal open spaces must not exceed 1.2 metres in height, are to be of solid masonry construction and integrated with any dividing walls for private open spaces at ground. Higher fencing may be considered on Windsor Road, subject to context analysis. Fencing on side boundaries is to be provided to a maximum height of 1.8 metres.

All balconies are to meet the minimum dimensions required in the NSW ADG. Wintergardens may be permitted on Windsor Road to improve the amenity of apartments fronting this arterial road. The floor space of the wintergarden will be excluded from the FSR calculations provided that it complies with and does not exceed the ADG.
Council may consider allowing greater building depths where this will not unnecessarily add to the bulk of any building and where high-quality building design, massing and articulation is achieved.
Landscaping and deep soil shall be provided in accordance with Part 2 – Design in Context of this DCP, Figure 8.5.10.2.9 and Figure 8.5.10.2.10.

A detailed landscape plan is to be prepared demonstrating the location of all contiguous deep soil areas with proposed landscaping, including retained and new canopy trees, submitted to the satisfaction of Council.
Deep soil is provided in a contiguous manner to facilitate healthy soils, uninterrupted flows of groundwater, and opportunity for existing and new trees to thrive and reach mature height.
Existing trees on the site are to be retained, as possible. Future development must not impinge on TPZ requirements of all trees in the neighbouring property must be protected and retained.
Landscaping should include endemic species suitable to the environmental constraints and orientation of communal open spaces shall be utilised throughout the site.
The front setback is to be planted with large shady trees capable of reaching a mature height of more than 13 metres to provide a visual buffer and shading of the public footpath along Windsor road.

The rear setback should be planted with large trees, capable of reaching a mature height of more than 13 metre to enable Council’s vision of providing mature trees and natural shade in the City. Any new trees are to be planted more than 3 m away from any built structure.
Dual basements contained within the building envelope are to be provided, ensuring substantial and contiguous deep soil zones to the front, centre and rear.

Future redevelopment of the site is to meet the requirements of Parramatta LEP 2023, Parramatta DCP 2023, State Environmental Planning Policy No. 55 (Remediation of Land) and any other relevant legislation and guidelines.
Detailed design development must have regard to the sensitivity to flooding impacts, not impede overland storm water flows and able to meet the requirements of Council’s Flood Plain
A report is to be submitted by a suitably qualified ecologist at Development Application stage demonstrating that there are no adverse impacts from the development.

A minimum setback of 1 metre is required between the northern boundary and the driveway to allow for landscaping while still maintaining site access at the northern most point of the site off Windsor Road.
Wintergardens must improve amenity of balconies in high rise apartments and apartments fronting noisy environments such as busy roads or railway lines.

Wintergardens are to be designed and constructed as a private external balcony with drainage, natural ventilation and finishes acceptable to an outdoor space and must not be treated as a conditioned space or weatherproof space.
Approximately 80% of vertical surface area of wintergardens are to be fully operable louvres or sliding glass panels.
A generous opening must be provided between the wintergarden and any adjacent living area to allow connection of the spaces when ambient conditions are suitable.
Acoustic control for living areas and bedrooms must be provided on the internal façade line between the wintergarden and the living area or bedroom.
Glazing in the external façade of a wintergarden must have a solar absorption of less than 35%glass to have solar heat absorption not greater than a clear float glass of the same composition.
The flooring of the wintergarden must be an impervious finish and provide exposed thermal mass.
Air conditioning units must not be located on wintergarden balconies. Solar access, ventilation & acoustic amelioration

Buildings are to be designed to ensure that solar access and cross ventilation requirements detailed in SEPP 65, the NSW Apartment Design Guide and Part 3 – Residential Development of this DCP are achieved for residential development both on and off the site.

Solar access must also be reasonably provided/retained within the existing and future public domain areas and on adjoining sites to maximise solar access in mid- winter and canopy cover and shading in mid-summer.

The design of buildings must take account of the need for adequate acoustic amelioration measures for new development, particularly where buildings have an interface with major roads, including Windsor Road and James Ruse Drive or other non-residential uses in proximity to the site.

rules.rule_title: Pedestrian connections and vehicular access way
rules.rule_text: New pedestrian and vehicular connections are to be provided in accordance with Figure 8.5.10.2.11.

rules.rule_title: Pedestrian connections and vehicular access way
rules.rule_text: The vehicular access way is to be designed to have a fully public nature equivalent to the surrounding public domain and suitably designed to integrate with adjoining road and pedestrian networks.

rules.rule_title: Pedestrian connections and vehicular access way
rules.rule_text: New pedestrian connections are to be provided between the buildings, to enable linkages to recreated to both Windsor Road and the future pedestrian/bike link to the east and improve the development interface and amenity with all adjacent properties and frontages.

rules.rule_title: Pedestrian connections and vehicular access way
rules.rule_text: All site circulation must be provided as 24hr publicly accessible circulation - designed to provide building entries that are easily identifiable, with a clear sense of building address for residents and their visitors.

rules.rule_title: Pedestrian connections and vehicular access way
rules.rule_text: Main building entry points must be clearly visible and signalled appropriately with building address, lighting and high-quality articulation. Steps, handrails or TGSIs must not protrude into or interfere with any vehicular or pedestrian access way.

rules.rule_title: Pedestrian connections and vehicular access way
rules.rule_text: The pedestrian link along the eastern boundary must be publicly accessible by 24/7 access easement in favour of Council in accordance with the Voluntary Planning Agreement prior to the first Occupation Certificate and is to be clearly delineated as public space.

rules.rule_title: Pedestrian connections and vehicular access way
rules.rule_text: New development is to be sited to appropriately integrate with and address pedestrian links ensuring activation and casual surveillance. Tall fencing is not to be provided adjacent to the pedestrian links.

rules.rule_title: Pedestrian connections and vehicular access way
rules.rule_text: All internal pedestrian systems shall incorporate access in accordance with AS 1428 and any other relevant standard. These pathways are to enable a future connection to the school from the development to the south to the external pedestrian system surrounding the site.

rules.rule_title: Pedestrian connections and vehicular access way
rules.rule_text: Public domain alignment drawings are to be submitted to the satisfaction of Council. All levels are to be resolved and proposed public domain treatments shown in accordance with the requirements outlined in the Parramatta Public Domain Guidelines 2017 (Chapter 2).

rules.rule_title: Pedestrian connections and vehicular access way
rules.rule_text: A minimum width of 9 metres is to be provided to the vehicular access way, inclusive of twoway vehicular access and at least one lane of parallel parking.

rules.rule_title: Pedestrian connections and vehicular access way
rules.rule_text: A minimum width of 1.8 metres is to be provided for all pedestrian pathways.

rules.rule_title: Pedestrian connections and vehicular access way
rules.rule_text: A minimum width of 2.8 metres is to be dedicated for the future pedestrian and cycleway shared path along the new vehicle access way and eastern boundary. It should be publicly accessible by a 24/7 access easement in favour of Council in accordance with the Voluntary Planning Agreement prior to first Occupation Certificate.

All car parking is to be provided at basement level to ensure that the visual appearance of car parking structures does not dominate the building design. Basement structures may not protrude any greater than 1 metre above natural ground.

Building services and access to car parking areas are to be minimised to the internal street frontages to ensure that a high level of design excellence is achieved and opportunities for passive surveillance are maximised. Pedestrian and vehicle conflict are to be minimised with limited vehicle crossings to the public domain and internal access way.
Vehicle crossings are to be provided in accordance with Figure 8.5.10.2.11 (above), or as otherwise agreed by Council.
Vehicle crossings must not provide conflict with pedestrian through site links or any pedestrian crossing.
The width and surface area of driveways and other hard surfaces associated with the movement and parking of vehicles is to enable 2 vehicles plus one lane of parallel parking.

Provision of loading bays or service vehicle areas, building service/plant areas, and building services (such as substation) must be adequately screened from any public domain areas, including the street, through site links.

The vehicular access way must maintain its potential to connect through the adjacent school to Campbell Street in the future. This access way is to terminate in a hammer head, rather than a cul-de-sac configuration, so as to maintain the visual continuity of this link and kerb lines.
The street layout, through-site links, open space, setbacks, and development sites are to be consistent with the Plan shown in Figure 8.5.11.2.
Privately-owned pedestrian connections and internal streets are to be publicly accessible 24/7.
Reinforce the appropriate spatial definition of streets and public spaces.
Emphasise the importance of the streets as a distinct spatial entity and design the street interface and street wall with an appropriate human scale and sense of enclosure for the street.
Ensure consistent street frontages with buildings having common setbacks and alignments.
Provide building forms that achieve comfortable public domain conditions for pedestrians, with adequate daylight, appropriate scale, and adequate mitigation of wind effects on tower buildings.
Create a clear delineation between public and private space.
Provide a landscape interface between buildings and streets, to enable deep soil and street tree planting.
Reinforce important elements of the local context, namely public open spaces, key attractions and landscape elements.
Protect daylight access at street level and permit views of sky from the street by providing setbacks above street frontage height that promote separation between buildings.

Tower forms should be designed so that they are visually and physically separated from the podium. Separation should be achieved by a combination of architectural expression and design, materiality and setbacks.
Building Setbacks (Podium)
Podium setbacks are to include deep soil landscaping to encourage the provision of vegetation softening the built forms.
Awnings are permitted to encroach the podium setbacks on active frontages interfacing with the public domain, where necessary, to improve the useability and amenity of the site.

Podium setbacks may include the encroachment of architectural elements and features to a depth of 600mm where they provide visual interest. This can include elements such as balconies, fins and the like.
Vehicle access and basement entries are to be wholly located within the building footprint and not encroach on the 10m Pennant Hill Road landscape setback.
Upper-Level Setbacks
Create appropriate transition of built form to adjoining development that responds to the topography and the wider locality.
Ensure that building forms provide a high level of residential amenity including to adjoining residential development.
Ensure the bulk and scale of podiums and towers respond to site topography and create a relatable human scale interface to the public domain.
Ensure the height of buildings allows for high levels of solar access to the public domain, view sharing and views to sky.
Ensure that the building form enables the provision of a safe and comfortable pedestrian level wind environment, including street frontages, outdoor eating areas, and open spaces.
Ensure height of buildings allows for an appropriate distribution of built form density and height differentiation across the site.
Maximise opportunities for public domain and residential amenity through appropriate distribution of height.
Minimise the impact of development on the public domain, neighbouring sites and between buildings within the site by allowing adequate daylight and views to the sky between buildings.
Provide access to light, air, and outlook for the occupants of buildings, neighbouring properties, and future buildings.
Ensure towers are sufficiently separated so that tower buildings are seen in the round.
Minimise the perception of visual bulk and scale of the development.
Provide for the amenity, interest and liveliness of the street environment.
Ensure a positive experience for pedestrians.
Provide an active ground floor frontage that is accessible and integrated with the design of the public domain.
Deliver buildings that are well-proportioned.
Create a high-quality landscaped setting.
Ensure materials contribute positively to the streetscape quality, are sustainable, durable, and easy to maintain.
Mitigate reflectivity impacts on motorists and pedestrians on Pennant Hills Road.
Only one step in the built form is preferred. This is to occur between the podium and upperlevel building elements, unless required to satisfy ADG and ensure solar access to adjoining properties.
Basements are to be located below ground.
Where a basement breaches the natural ground level, it should be set back to be located within building footprints.
Where parking cannot be accommodated below ground level, this is to be sleeved with active retail or residential uses.
Buildings are to have a high level of articulation at both podium and tower levels.
Buildings shall implement a variety of high quality, sustainable, durable and coherent materials in a range of compatible colours and textures.
The design of podiums shall achieve the following outcomes:
The design of towers shall achieve the following:
Provide appropriate amenity for buildings.
Ensure that floor heights support a range of uses and enable a change of use over time.
Deliver a ground floor that achieves amenity and privacy for residents as well as engagement with and passive surveillance of the street.
Maximise deep soil and green landscape area in the 4m internal street setback providing a dominant landscape setting for new buildings.
Provide appropriate amenity for all residential apartments.
Locate the disability access so that it relates seamlessly to the building design.
Minimise the impact of basements.
Preserve landscaped site boundary setback areas as deep soil area that can support significant tree vegetation commensurate with the proposed scale of development.
Preserve boundary setback areas predominantly at natural ground level avoiding the need for large retaining structures or steep embankments abutting neighbouring properties or existing streets.
Ensure suitable conditions for access, plant establishment, and convenient long-term maintenance of landscaped areas.
Ground floor apartments should be adjacent to footpath levels as far as practicable. Where this is not achievable, they should still achieve a high level of amenity.

Where apartment have individual entries from the street, a front door with a distinct entry space within the apartment should be provided. Individual apartment entries should be understated, with post boxes and street numbers located at the common entry.
The setback area should be designed to relate to the public footpath and maximise landscaping area.
A variety of landscaping, including canopy trees, should be provided within setbacks.
Minimise impervious surfaces at ground level in the setback areas.
Gradient change/embankment should be no more than 1:6.
Building floor plates and sections should define positive spaces for streets, open spaces, and courtyards.
High-level windows should not be used as the primary source of light and ventilation for habitable rooms.
Where practicable, balconies should be rectangular in shape with the longer side parallel to the facade of the building.
Divisions between apartment balconies should be of solid, non-transparaent construction and extend from floor to ceiling.
Common open space should include appropriate facilities for the use of residents.
Balustrades should take account of sightlines to balance the need for privacy within apartments and views out of apartments.
Apartment design should consider incorporating suitable spaces that can be utilised as a work from home space.
Promote an appropriate mixture of uses which will support vitality of the site and surrounding locality, including public open spaces and existing and future road network.
Encourage a range of non-residential uses that meet the needs of local residents.
Ensure that safe and convenient car parking arrangements for childcare facilities are provided and avoid adverse traffic and on-street parking impacts on the surrounding neighbourhood.
Ensure that commercial uses do not unreasonably diminish the amenity of nearby residential uses through noise intrusion.
Provide active ground floor uses along street frontages, through site links to create an active pedestrian edge as well as maximising opportunities for passive surveillance.
The non-residential uses are to be provided to meet the needs of the community and focused around the periphery of the central public open space and through-site links, activating the locality.
Where necessary, the fit out and use of non-residential components should form part of separate applications.

Where non-residential uses are proposed on the site, consideration must be given to ensure appropriate amelioration measures are considered with regard to noise, odours and the like to reduce conflict with residential development.
Provide a prominent, accessible, and appropriately located facility comprising of a co-located community centre and library that meets the needs of the Carlingford community and visitors.
Create a vibrant facility that is integrated into the adjacent outdoor spaces and commercial, retail, and public domain areas.
Incorporate best practice sustainability principles and standards into the design of the building and operation of the facility.
Ensure the community centre and library facility can provide refuge and evacuation support during extreme weather and emergency events.
Provide activated surrounding public spaces and landscapes that establish a community focus.
Ensure that the facility does not impact the amenity of surrounding residents.
Ensure adequate on-site car parking is provided for the facility.
The library/community centre provides an active interface to the central park and east-west through link.
The facility is to be provided on the subject site within Block F as per the site layout in Figure 8.5.11.2.
The library space is to be a minimum area of 1,800m2 measured from internal walls of the external building.
The community facility space is to be a minimum area of 700m2 measured from the internal walls of the external building.
Windows are not to be more than 300mm below ceiling height.
Vehicular entry is to be provided to the facility in accordance with Figure 8.5.11.7.
Two pedestrian access points are to be provided, one from the through site link.

The community facility is to have an active interface with Shirley Street, the new internal street and the through-site link. These frontages should also include awnings. Awnings are to be designed in accordance with Section 8.5.11.23 – Awnings and Awning Design of this DCP.
Building services and plant rooms shall not create a negative impact on the amenity of users or residents and are to be located away from prominent public domain and residential interfaces.
Services should not impact on minimum floor to ceiling heights.

Building design should incorporate high quality materials and finishes and be of high sustainability value. Sustainability measures are to applied in accordance with the Sustainability within this site-specific DCP.
On-site car parking provision is to be in accordance with the rate specified in the table within C.09 of Section 8.5.11.26 – On-site Parking of this DCP.
Floor to floor heights are to be as per C.09 of Section 8.5.11.8 – Floor to Floor Heights of this DCP.
Provide a significant presentation, interface and outlook for all parts of the community hub with an elevation facing the central park and the east-west through link.
Improve the amenity of balconies in high-rise apartments above eight storeys and apartments fronting noisy environments.
Provide acoustic attenuation for internal living areas.
Provide an acceptable thermal environment.
Balance ventilation and wind impacts in high-rise apartment balconies.
Maximise daylight access, views, and comfort of balconies.
Wintergardens are only permitted above 8 storeys (including the eighth storey) or where there are negative external impacts such as high levels of noise.

Wintergardens should be designed and constructed as a private external balcony with drainage, natural ventilation, and finishes acceptable to an outdoor space and should not be treated as a conditioned space or weatherproof space.

All wintergardens are to have a balustrade less than 1.4m above finished floor level and a contiguous and permanently openable area between the balustrade and the ceiling level of not less than 25% of this area. This restriction shall apply to all elevations if the wintergarden has multiple elevations.
A generous opening should be provided between the wintergarden and any adjacent living area to allow connection of the spaces when ambient conditions are suitable.
Acoustic control for living areas and bedrooms should be provided on the internal facade line between the wintergarden and the living area or bedroom.
Winter gardens should have 75% of the external walls (excluding balustrade) fully operable louvres or sliding glass panels. Casement or awning windows are not permitted.
Air conditioning units should not be located on wintergarden balconies.
A Site Water Management Master Plan (WMP) must be submitted with any development application on the site and agreed to by Council.
The WMP Plan shall guide water aspects of development and infrastructure, landscape and environment in the precinct and must include:

A piped drainage reticulation system capable of carrying the 1 in 20 chance per year stormwater flows is to be provided throughout the site for all roads and public domain areas. This system must be designed and constructed to Council standards and specifications and reasonable satisfaction. Where appropriate, public drainage infrastructure shall be dedicated to Council at appropriate stages in the development process for ongoing operation by Council.

Excess peak flows from private lots, public roads and public domain shall be detained in both on-site and collective detention systems as appropriate. Detention systems are to be integrated into a sustainable overall water management plan for the site which may include WSD and rainwater harvesting. Peak flows are to be limited throughout the catchment in a 1 in 100 chance per year storm event to estimated peak flows under 1999 conditions. Detention design and details shall be in accordance with the UPRCT Handbook Edition 4.

Lower flows (up to 1 chance in 1.5 years) shall be managed using water sensitive design methods primarily within the landscape and directed through landscape water quality biotreatment systems including deep soil and bioretention.

Each proposal for private development and for public infrastructure and public domain development must be supported by a Water Management Plan that addresses the water aspects of the proposal, and the affected landscape and environment and is consistent with the WMP and is satisfactory to Council. Each proposal must address:
Tanked (waterproofed) basements are preferred, drained basements may be permitted where captured groundwater can be re-used on-site.

The role of open space in water management design and management must be clearly demonstrated in the Water Management Plans. Recreational functionality must be compatible with and not unduly restrict or be restricted by any stormwater management requirements in the public domain and open spaces. The use of well-designed water management facilities, such as ponds, streams and wetlands, to enhance recreation and amenity is encouraged.
